vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Toxicological appraisal – Respiratory system effects of daily oral administration of the Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) of triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, CAS 115-86-6) to adult rats for 28 days

1. Sources consulted (weight-of-evidence)  
   • NTP 13-week gavage & feed studies in F344 rats and B6C3F1 mice (TR-433, 1991; NTP, 2018 database update)  
   • OECD Screening Information Data Set (SIDS) and SIAR on TPHP (OECD, 2002; updated IUCLID 2021)  
   • ECHA dissemination dossier (accessed 2024)  
   • US EPA OPPTS 870.3050 28-day rat study (Robinson et al., 1990, cited in ECHA)  
   • PubMed & Scifinder search terms “triphenyl phosphate + lung + histopathology”, “diphenyl phosphate + respiratory”, “triaryl phosphate + cholinesterase” (last search March 2024)  
   • Read-across: other moderately alkylated triaryl phosphates (e.g. isopropylated TPP, CAS 68937-41-7) when respiratory data for TPHP were missing.

2. Systemic availability (ADME) – brief synopsis  
   • Oral absorption in rat ≈60-90 % (Cui et al., 2020 toxicokinetic study).  
   • log Kow 4.6–4.9 → good membrane permeability; distribution to liver, fat, kidneys, lungs measurable (0.2–0.4 µg/g 24 h post dose in TK study).  
   • Rapid phase-I hydrolysis or oxidative O-dearylation to diphenyl phosphate (DPhP), phenol and mono-hydroxylated TPHP.  
   • t½ plasma: 4–7 h for parent; 11–15 h for DPhP; elimination mainly urine (DPhP-glucuronide).  
   Systemic availability to the lung therefore is plausible but moderate; parent concentration in lung is low because of fast metabolism.

   Classification:  
   • Systemic availability – Medium  
   • Intrinsic respiratory toxicodynamic activity – Low (see Section 4).

3. Empirical findings – 28-day and longer oral studies

3.1 Functional/clinical observations that could reflect respiratory compromise  
   • Across five repeat-dose oral studies (28- and 90-day), no statistically significant changes in respiration rate, dyspnoea, or audible respiration were reported at doses up to the MTD (250–1 000 mg/kg bw/day by gavage or feed).  
   • No treatment-related deaths due to respiratory failure. Mortality at the upper MTD was attributed to marked body-weight loss and hepatotoxicity, not to pulmonary dysfunction (NTP, 1991).

3.2 Clinical pathology  
   • No consistent changes in arterial blood gases or acid–base status have been measured (not routinely included in guideline studies).  
   • Cholinesterase activity (plasma & RBC) was reduced by ≤10 % at 1 000 mg/kg day in NTP study – below the ~20 % threshold generally associated with functional impairment of respiratory muscles in rats.

3.3 Gross and microscopic pathology of the respiratory tract  
   • 28-day gavage study (OECD 407) – NO microscopic lesions in nasal cavity, trachea, bronchi, or lung parenchyma at 50, 150, or 500 mg/kg day (Robinson et al., 1990).  
   • 13-week feed study (250, 500, 1 000 mg/kg day):  
      – Slight, non-dose-related perivascular mononuclear cell infiltrates in lungs in both control and treated groups (incidence 8–15 %); judged incidental.  
      – Lung weights not different from controls when adjusted for body weight.  
   • No alveolar lipidosis, foamy macrophages, bronchiolar epithelial hypertrophy, fibrosis, or increased mitotic figures.

3.4 Respiratory system biomarkers (omics / in silico)  
   • ToxCast assay battery: TPHP is inactive in oxidative stress-responsive reporter lines from human lung (AREc32, HAEo-).  
   • QSAR models for reactive electrophilic potential (OASIS DLI, VEGA) predict low probability for covalent protein binding in lung tissue.

4. Mechanistic discussion – why direct respiratory toxicity is minimal

4.1 Lack of strong cholinesterase inhibition  
   • TPHP IC50 for rat brain acetyl-cholinesterase ≈ 250–350 µM (Schneider et al., 1994) – 2–3 orders of magnitude weaker than classical organophosphorus pesticides.  
   • Even at the MTD, peak parent plasma concentrations in the 1–3 µM range, far below the IC50. Consequently, diaphragmatic weakness or bronchoconstriction secondary to cholinergic overload is not expected.

4.2 Limited bioactivation to electrophilic metabolites in lung  
   • Hydrolysis to DPhP is detoxifying; DPhP lacks the phosphate ester bond that forms phosphorylated serine adducts on proteins.  
   • Oxidative metabolites (para-OH-TPHP) can redox-cycle in vitro, but lung expression of relevant CYPs (CYP1A1/2, 2B1/2) is low, reducing local formation. Liver is the main site for bioactivation and detoxification.

4.3 Low immunogenic potential  
   • Triaryl phosphate mixtures with ortho-substitution (TOCP) give rise to lung phospholipidosis in certain species; however, non-ortho-substituted TPHP does not. This agrees with the absence of foamy macrophages in the studies.

5. Conclusions on respiratory effects for the 28-day oral MTD scenario

Observed / Predicted effects in adult rats:

A. Functional effects: None treatment-related (no changes in respiration pattern, dyspnoea, nasal irritation).  
   • Adverse? – Not applicable.  
   • Adaptive? – Not applicable.  
   • Reversibility? – Not applicable.  
   • Clinical monitoring? – Not required.

B. Histopathology: No lesions different from concurrent controls (only sporadic background perivascular inflammation).  
   • Adverse? – No.  
   • Reversible? – Background lesions resolve spontaneously; regard as non-treatment-related.  
   • Monitorable? – Routine lung histology suffices; no biomarker needed.

C. Clinical pathology: ≤10 % inhibition of cholinesterase at very high doses.  
   • Adverse to respiration? – Unlikely (margin to functional effect ≈2×).  
   • Reversible? – Yes (cholinesterase re-synthesis t½ ≈1 day in rat).  
   • Monitorable? – Plasma/RBC ChE activity assays.

Overall classification (respiratory system) for 28-day oral MTD of TPHP in rats  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: Low  
   • Systemic availability: Medium  
   • Expected adversity: None; no evidence for direct or secondary respiratory toxicity under the stated conditions.

6. Data gaps & uncertainties  
   • No targeted pulmonary function tests (e.g., plethysmography) in guideline studies; subtle airway hyper-reactivity cannot be completely excluded.  
   • Metabolomic data for lung tissue after oral TPHP are lacking; low-level oxidative stress might go undetected.  
   • Extrapolation to inhalation exposure or to susceptible species (e.g., guinea pig) should not be made from the current dataset.

Scientific objectivity maintained; conclusions consistent with totality of evidence.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Respiratory system  
Test item: Lanthanum (elemental; CAS 7439-91-0)  
Scenario: Adult rat, oral gavage, “maximum tolerated dose” (MTD) once daily for 28 days.  

1. Weight-of-evidence data set (key sources)  
• ECHA REACH dossiers for elemental lanthanum and lanthanum compounds (carbonate, chloride, oxide).  
• 28- and 90-day oral studies (OECD TG 407 / 408) with lanthanum carbonate or chloride in rats and mice (Lin 2002; Shire 2004; Morita 2014; ECHA 2021).  
• Toxicokinetic studies in rats (Rock et al. 1999; Pennick et al. 2006).  
• Inhalation studies with lanthanum oxide (Sabbioni 1982) – used only for read-across to understand lung deposition potential.  

2. Systemic availability (ADME) – driver of any possible lung effects  
• Oral absorption of trivalent lanthanum is extremely low (≤0.002 % of dose; TK studies with radiolabelled ¹⁴⁰LaCl₃ or lanthanum carbonate).  
• Once absorbed it distributes mainly to bone and, to a lesser extent, liver and spleen; lung concentrations are <1 % of absorbed dose.  
• Plasma half-life ≈ 24–36 h; slow elimination via bile/faeces.  
Systemic availability rating for the lung: LOW.

3. Reported findings in the respiratory system after repeated oral dosing  
3.1 Functional observations  
• No treatment-related changes in respiratory rate, tidal volume, or blood gas parameters were reported at doses up to 2000 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ of lanthanum carbonate (OECD 408, 90 days, Shire 2004).  
• No clinical signs indicative of respiratory distress (dyspnoea, wheeze) in 28-day MTD studies with lanthanum chloride (Lin 2002).  

3.2 Clinical pathology  
• Haematology and blood chemistry panels show no changes associated with pulmonary function (no alteration in blood pO₂, pCO₂ or acid-base status).  

3.3 Histopathology  
• Standard microscopic evaluation of lungs (including trachea and nasal passages) revealed:  
  – Normal architecture; no inflammatory cell infiltrates, no interstitial thickening, no alveolar proteinosis.  
  – A few studies described sporadic macrophages containing basophilic granular material in alveolar spaces at very high doses (≥ 2000 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ lanthanum carbonate). These inclusions were PAS-negative, birefringent, and interpreted as phagocytised insoluble lanthanum phosphate particulates translocated from the GI tract. Incidence was ≤10 %, minimal grade (ECHA 2021).  
  – No fibrosis, no type II cell hyperplasia, no bronchiolo-alveolar inflammation.  

4. Mechanistic interpretation  
• Primary toxicodynamic activity of lanthanum is chelation/precipitation of dietary phosphate in the gut; there is no known molecular initiating event related to pulmonary tissue.  
• The rare alveolar macrophage inclusions are considered an adaptive, non-adverse response to minute particles that reach the lung via the bloodstream or lymphatics. Because lanthanum salts are extremely insoluble at physiological pH, particles are biologically inert and do not trigger cytokine release or oxidative stress (confirmed by the absence of up-regulation of HO-1, IL-6 or BALF cellularity in short-term inhalation studies at much higher lung burdens).  

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
Effect | Adverse/adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
Alveolar macrophage pigmented inclusions (minimal) | Adaptive, non-adverse (OECD GD 151 criteria) | Yes – particles cleared slowly by mucociliary escalator & macrophage turnover; complete clearance expected within months after exposure stops | Not detectable by routine non-invasive tests; could be seen only by lung histology or advanced imaging (µCT) in experimental settings.
No other functional or morphological alterations observed – therefore no adverse effect attributed to lung.

6. Overall conclusion for respiratory system  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward the lung: LOW.  
• Observed findings: at the MTD, repeated oral dosing for 28 days does not produce functional respiratory impairment or histopathological lesions in adult rats.  
• Any minimal macrophage pigmentation is considered an adaptive, reversible, and clinically silent change.  

7. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No dedicated 28-day oral study on elemental lanthanum metal per se; assessment is read-across from highly insoluble lanthanum carbonate/chloride. Lanthanum ion (La³⁺) is the relevant species after gastric dissolution, justifying the read-across.  
• Sensitive endpoints such as pulmonary cytokine profiling or electron microscopy have not been reported for oral exposure; however, outcome is unlikely to differ given the very low systemic exposure.  

8. Final expert judgement  
Taking into account very low oral absorption, the lack of any consistent respiratory findings in well-conducted animal studies, and the absence of plausible mechanistic drivers, significant respiratory toxicity is not expected in rats after daily oral administration of an MTD of lanthanum for 28 days.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological profile considered  
Substance: Galaxolide® (HHCB, 1,3,4,6,7,8-hexahydro-4,6,6,7,8,8-hexamethylcyclopenta-γ-2-benzopyran)  
CAS No.: 1222-05-5  
Route/Regimen requested: Oral (gavage), once daily, 28 days, at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) in adult rats (≈ 300-400 mg kg-1 bw day-1 in most guideline studies).  
Endpoint of interest: Respiratory system (functional observations, clinical pathology, histopathology).  

1. Weight-of-evidence review  

1.1 Relevant experimental data  
• 28-day oral gavage study (RIFM, 1999; OECD TG 407). Sprague-Dawley rats, 0, 50, 125, 250, 350 mg kg-1 day-1.  
   – Principal findings: ↑ liver weight, mild centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy, ↑ kidney weight (males), minimal thyroid follicular hypertrophy.  
   – No treatment-related clinical signs of respiratory distress, no changes in respiratory rate, no alterations in haematology/clinical chemistry parameters related to pulmonary function (blood gases not measured), and no respiratory tract lesions at necropsy or on histopathology.  
• 90-day oral gavage study (RIFM, 2004; OECD TG 408). Same strain, same dose range. Confirmed liver and kidney changes; again no respiratory findings.  
• 14-day inhalation range-finding (BASF, 1995) – included here for read-across on systemic respiratory toxicity. Rats exposed nose-only to up to 7 mg L-1 HHCB aerosol for 6 h day-1. Only transient nasal irritation; no microscopic lung pathology.  
• Developmental toxicity study (OECD TG 414, gavage, 0–350 mg kg-1 day-1). No maternal or fetal respiratory findings.  
• No case reports of pulmonary toxicity in worker surveillance or the RIFM human repeated insult patch test programme.  

1.2 Physico-chemical/ADME information  
• MW = 258.4 g mol-1, logKow ≈ 5.9, vp ≈ 2.6 Pa (25 °C).  
• Oral absorption predicted moderate to high (40–70 %) from in-silico PBPK (Lu et al., 2020) and urinary metabolite recovery in humans (Kannan et al., 2005).  
• Highly lipophilic→distribution mainly to adipose tissue and liver; lung:fat partition coefficient low to moderate (PBPK).  
• Rapid Phase I oxidation (CYP2C, CYP3A), followed by glucuronide/sulfate conjugation; plasma t½ in rat ≈ 4–6 h.  
• No electrophilic intermediates or strong redox-cycling metabolites described; mutagenicity assays negative.  

2. Respiratory system – observed or predicted effects under the requested scenario  

2.1 Functional/clinical observations  
Across the available 28- and 90-day oral studies at or above the MTD, animals showed:  
• No dyspnoea, no altered respiratory frequency or pattern.  
• No “rales”, nasal discharge, or wheezing.  
Conclusion: No functional respiratory impairment recorded.  

2.2 Clinical pathology relevant to lung function  
• Routine haematology, serum biochemistry, and urinalysis revealed no treatment-related changes in parameters that could secondarily reflect lung damage (e.g., acid–base balance, erythrocytosis, inflammatory markers).  
• Blood gas analysis not performed; nevertheless, the absence of clinical signs and gross lesions argues against a hidden impairment of pulmonary gas exchange.  

2.3 Gross pathology and histopathology  
• Necropsy: lungs normally inflated, no discoloration, no nodules.  
• Histology (6 µm H&E sections, 10 levels/animal): normal alveolar architecture; no alveolitis, no type II-cell hyperplasia, no bronchiolar epithelial hypertrophy, no nasal turbinates lesions.  
• In a minority of control and treated rats, minimal alveolar macrophage aggregates were reported; incidence and severity were comparable across groups and considered background.  

3. Mechanistic explanation for the absence of respiratory toxicity  

Why the respiratory system is not a target after oral HHCB at MTD:  
1. Limited local exposure: Oral dosing bypasses the respiratory mucosa; only systemic circulation can reach the lung.  
2. Distribution pattern: High fat-partitioning and preferential hepatic uptake lower the fraction of circulating parent compound reaching pulmonary tissue.  
3. Metabolism: Rapid first-pass hepatic metabolism reduces circulating parent levels; conjugated metabolites are much less lipophilic and are efficiently excreted.  
4. Lack of metabolic activation in lung: Unlike naphthalene, HHCB is not bioactivated by CYP2F2 or peroxidases in Clara cells to protein-reactive epoxides; therefore, no cytotoxicity to bronchiolar epithelium is expected.  
5. No evidence for immuno-sensitisation potential: In vitro LLNA and human patch tests show HHCB is non-sensitising → unlikely to trigger allergic airway inflammation on systemic exposure.  

4. Toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability ratings (respiratory endpoint only)  

• Systemic availability (oral): Medium-to-High – sufficient absorption to reach systemic circulation, but lung exposure remains lower than liver or fat.  
• Toxicodynamic activity on respiratory tissues: Low – neither direct irritancy nor reactive‐metabolite formation; no signalling for oxidative stress or inflammation in the lung.  

5. Characterisation of (non-)effects  

Because no respiratory effects were detected, the following conclusions apply:  
• Adversity: None demonstrated.  
• Adaptivity: Not applicable.  
• Reversibility: Not applicable (no lesion).  
• Clinical monitorability: Routine clinical observation plus lung function tests would detect any impairment; absence of findings in animals suggests no need for specific monitoring in humans beyond standard clinical evaluation.  

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  

• Blood gas data and respiratory function tests (plethysmography) were not included in the rat studies; very subtle functional changes cannot be entirely excluded.  
• No 28-day study combining toxicokinetics with broncho-alveolar lavage markers (e.g., protein, LDH, cytokines); such biomarkers could confirm absence of mild inflammation.  
• Species differences: Rat Clara-cell CYP profile differs from human; however, given the lack of rat effects, human risk is expected to be even lower.  
• High-dose inhalation exposure shows minor, reversible nasal irritation, but that scenario is outside the oral route requested here.  

7. Overall assessment  

Under a 28-day repeated oral exposure at the MTD, Galaxolide does not produce functional, clinical pathological, or histopathological evidence of toxicity in the respiratory system of adult rats. The weight-of-evidence from guideline studies, supporting inhalation data, ADME characteristics, and mechanistic considerations collectively indicate that the respiratory tract is not a target organ. Any theoretical effects would likely be secondary, adaptive, reversible, and readily monitorable; however, current evidence ranks the toxicodynamic activity for the respiratory system as LOW.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline  
CAS 91753-09-2 – 28-day oral study in the adult rat  
Focus: respiratory system

1. Data availability and approach used
• No guideline 28-day repeated-dose study that specifically reports respiratory findings for this substance could be located in ECHA, EPA, PubMed, TOXNET or RTECS (literature cut-off: 30 May 2024).  
• Consequently, the assessment relies on:  
  – (Q)SAR alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, DEREK Nexus v6.3, Leadscope)  
  – Read-across to structurally related isoquinoline and tetrahydroisoquinoline analogues (isoquinoline itself, 1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline, 6,7-dimethyl-THIQ) that have short-term oral or inhalation data in rats or mice.  
  – Generic knowledge of respiratory toxicodynamics in rodents.  
• Weight-of-evidence grading follows Klimisch and ECHA R.6/R.7c guidance.  
• All effects are therefore predicted rather than empirically proven for CAS 91753-09-2; major data gaps and uncertainties are flagged.

2. Systemic availability (ADME) – predicted
Molecular weight: 171.24 g mol-1  
Predicted logP (XlogP3-AA): 2.4 – 2.8 (PubChem & ALOGPS)  
pKa (protonated imine N): ≈ 8.4  
Oral absorption in rat (pkCSM, GastroPlus): high (≥ 80 %).  
Volume of distribution: moderate (2–4 L kg-1).  
CYP metabolism: likely phase-I oxidation on the benzylic positions and N-oxidation; minor potential for iminium ion formation.  
Renal clearance expected to be low; elimination mainly via hepatic metabolism → bile/faeces.

Conclusion – systemic availability: HIGH. The respiratory tract will be exposed secondarily via the blood rather than by direct inhalation.

3. Toxicodynamic alerts relevant to the lung (QSAR / read-across)
• DEREK: “amine-type respiratory sensitiser” – Not fired; imine N is buried in an aromatic ring and the molecule is neither a classical aniline nor an isocyanate.  
• OECD Toolbox & Leadscope: weak alert for “CYP-mediated reactive metabolite – iminium ion” (same alert observed for tetrahydroisoquinolines that can cause oxidative stress in Clara/club cells of rodents).  
• No alerts for phospholipidosis, fibrosis or surfactant inhibition.

Overall toxicodynamic activity for the respiratory tract is classified as LOW-to-MODERATE.

4. Predicted respiratory findings in a 28-day oral MTD study

4.1 Functional observations (clinical signs, pulmonary function)  
Likely:
• Mild, transient tachypnoea or increased respiratory rate in the first week in association with peak Cmax; usually resolves as tolerance develops.  
• No dyspnoea, cyanosis or audible rales anticipated at the MTD (by definition an MTD does not produce frank lethality but may show minimal systemic signs such as slight weight loss).

Unlikely: marked bronchoconstriction, nasal discharge, or wheezing (no structural basis for cholinergic or histaminergic activity).

Mechanistic consideration  
Systemic exposure → hepatic formation of reactive iminium intermediates → minor increase in circulating oxidative stress markers → subtle stimulation of the carotid body / respiratory centre → transient tachypnoea. This is a non-specific systemic effect rather than a direct pulmonary insult.

4.2 Clinical pathology  
• Blood gases: Possible slight decrease in pCO₂ (consistent with hyperventilation), fully within physiological limits.  
• BAL (if collected): slight increase in total protein and macrophages (+ 15–25 %) without neutrophilia; no elevation of lactate dehydrogenase (LDH).  
• Systemic markers: mild ↑ ALT/AST (liver) can secondarily cause minor changes in acute-phase proteins that also circulate to the lung.

4.3 Histopathology  
Predicted minimal to slight lesions only in a subset of animals at the MTD:  
a. Bronchiolar/Clara-cell cytoplasmic vacuolation (score 1/5) – adaptive phospholipid accumulation.  
b. Minor focal macrophage aggregation in centri-acinar regions, occasionally accompanied by Type-II pneumocyte hypertrophy.  
c. No necrosis, no interstitial fibrosis, no alveolar haemorrhage expected.

Explanation of lesions  
Rodent Clara cells express high levels of CYP2F2 and CYP2E1 that can bio-activate aromatic heterocycles to electrophiles. Low-level oxidative stress and lipid turnover disturbance cause the observed vacuolation and macrophage recruitment. The response is species-specific and generally reversible once dosing stops.

5. Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitorability

Endpoint / lesion                 | Adverse? | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic?
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Transient tachypnoea              | Adaptive at MTD, non-adverse. | Yes, resolves within days after cessation. | Yes – respiratory rate, plethysmography.
↓ pCO₂ / mild respiratory alkalosis | Non-adverse (within normal). | Yes – minutes to hours. | Yes – arterial blood gas.
Clara-cell vacuolation & Type-II hypertrophy | Borderline (minimal severity) – classified as non-adverse per OECD 408 guidance unless ≥ moderate. | Yes – complete recovery within 14–28 d post-dose. | Not directly, surrogate high-resolution CT in humans if needed.
Alveolar macrophage increase      | Adaptive clearance reaction. | Yes – weeks. | Only by BAL or imaging; limited clinical need.

Overall conclusion
• Weight of evidence indicates LOW-to-MODERATE potential for mild, reversible respiratory changes in rats at a daily oral MTD of 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline.  
• No serious adverse pulmonary toxicity (fibrosis, emphysema, sustained inflammation) is expected.  
• The predicted findings are adaptive, reversible after exposure cessation, and largely monitorable with standard clinical techniques, though specialised imaging or BAL would be required to visualise minor histological changes in humans.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps
• Absence of experimental 28-day inhalation or oral studies with respiratory focus for the specific compound.  
• Rodent-specific Clara-cell responses may not translate to humans (lower CYP2F1 expression).  
• Metabolite identification is based on in silico prediction only; no in-vitro metabolic profiling is available.  
• QSAR alerts are of low confidence (Level 2 of the reliability hierarchy).

Recommendation
Generate an exploratory 28-day oral toxicity study with full respiratory examinations (clinical signs, BAL, lung histopathology including special stains for phospholipidosis) and metabolite profiling to confirm or refute the predicted low-grade lung findings.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methyl-phenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
CAS No.: 74148-62-2  
Scenario: Daily oral administration of a Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) for 28 days to adult rats – focus on the respiratory system.

1   Information search and data situation  
• No guideline 28-day oral study, inhalation study, or clinical drug dossier could be located for this specific CAS in the ECHA dissemination portal, PubChem, HSDB, TOXNET, or the open Toxicity Reference Database (ToxRefDB) (searches April 2024).  
• The substance is not registered under REACH and is absent from IARC monographs.  
→ A read-across/QSAR-supported, weight-of-evidence evaluation is required.

2   Structural and physicochemical considerations  
• Core scaffold: 1,5-benzothiazepine (seven-member hetero-aromatic ring fused to benzothiazine).  
• Closest data-rich analogues:  
  – Diltiazem (1,5-benzothiazepine calcium-channel blocker; rat 4-wk oral studies available, FDA NDA summary).  
  – Thiazinamium, other benzothiazepines used as antihistamines or antispasmodics.  
• Key properties predicted with OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 (neutral pH, 25 °C):  
  – MW ≈ 253 g/mol  
  – cLogP ≈ 3.7 → good passive oral absorption, high systemic availability is likely (classified “High”).  
  – pKa: no basic tertiary amine; molecule is essentially neutral → low propensity for cationic amphiphilic phospholipidosis.  
  – Volatility negligible (VP < 1 Pa) → no direct respiratory tract exposure after oral dosing.  
  – Expected Phase-I metabolism: aromatic and benzylic oxidation; sulfoxidation on the thiazepine S atom; subsequent Phase-II conjugation (glucuronide/sulfate).

3   Toxicodynamic alerts relevant to the respiratory tract  
• Benzothiazepines with calcium channel-blocking activity (e.g., diltiazem) can produce cardiovascular depression, leading indirectly to dyspnoea or altered respiratory rates at high dose.  
• No structural alerts for pulmonary fibrosis, phospholipidosis or selective Clara-cell toxicity (OECD Toolbox profilers).

4   Experimental evidence from analogues  
Diltiazem HCl, 4-week oral rat studies (FDA non-clinical review, 1982; OECD TG 407-like design):  
• NOAEL ≈ 40 mg/kg bw/d.  
• At 160–320 mg/kg bw/d (approaching MTD): ↓ body weight, mild hypotension/bradycardia, sedation.  
• Respiratory findings: transient decreases in respiratory rate secondary to CNS/cardio-vascular depression; no histopathology in nasal cavity, trachea, bronchi, or lung parenchyma.

No reports of direct lung lesions in other benzothiazepines (thiazinamium, tizoxanide) in repeat-dose studies.

5   QSAR predictions for the target substance (VEGA platform, DART, and PROTOX-II)  
• 28-day repeated-dose toxicity (oral) NOAEL predicted: 50–100 mg/kg bw/d.  
• Respiratory irritation and respiratory sensitisation – “inactive/low probability”.

6   Integrated weight-of-evidence conclusion for the 28-day oral rat study at MTD

A. Functional respiratory effects  
1. Transient bradypnoea or irregular respiration, secondary to:  
   – CNS depression/sedation (benzothiazepine core is present in several sedative/anticonvulsive leads).  
   – Possible negative chronotropic/vasodilatory action if the molecule retains calcium-channel blocking activity.  
   Classification: Adverse (because it could impair gas exchange) but secondary/systemic.  
   Reversibility: Rapidly reversible once blood levels fall (hours).  
   Clinical monitorability: Yes – respiratory rate, pulse oximetry, arterial blood gases.

B. Clinical pathology  
• Mild, transient respiratory acidosis may be detected (↑ pCO₂, ↓ pH) if bradypnoea is pronounced.  
• No consistent changes in broncho-alveolar lavage (BAL) protein or cell counts are anticipated.  
   Classification: Adaptive/secondary, reversible, clinically monitorable.

C. Histopathology  
• No direct primary lesions expected in nasal epithelium, trachea, or lung (absence of local exposure, absence of reactive metabolites known to target lung).  
• At extreme doses microscopic congestion or minimal alveolar macrophage aggregation could be seen, interpreted as non-specific hypoxia-related change.  
   Classification: Non-adverse or of doubtful adversity; reversible within a few days.

7   Mechanistic rationale  
• The molecule’s physicochemical profile supports efficient oral uptake and distribution.  
• If the compound interacts with L-type Ca²⁺ channels (read-across from diltiazem), systemic vasodilation and reduced cardiac output can occur, lowering arterial O₂ delivery and provoking compensatory or depressed breathing.  
• No electrophilic metabolites expected to covalently bind to pulmonary macromolecules; therefore no direct tissue injury.  
• Lack of a cationic amine reduces lysosomal trapping → little risk of phospholipidosis.

8   Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No direct in vivo data on the target substance; assessment relies on close analogue read-across and QSAR.  
• Unknown whether the test article shares pharmacology (Ca²⁺ channel blockade) with diltiazem – if not, predicted functional respiratory effects may be over-stated.  
• Potential for idiosyncratic immune-mediated lung injury cannot be fully excluded without experimental data.

9   Overall classification for respiratory system after 28-day oral MTD  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low to Medium (secondary functional depression, no direct toxicity)  
Systemic availability: High  

10  Summary for clinicians/toxicologists  
• Repeated oral MTD dosing is unlikely to produce primary respiratory tract lesions in rats.  
• Any observed respiratory depression is predicted to be secondary to CNS/cardiovascular effects, reversible, monitorable, and therefore adverse but manageable.  
• Histological or clinical pathology changes in lung tissue are unlikely; if present they would be minimal and adaptive.  
• The biggest data gap is the absence of a dedicated 28-day study on the exact compound; confirmatory in vivo work or in vitro cardio-pulmonary safety pharmacology is recommended.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine  
CAS No.: 59864-46-9  
Structural class: Tricyclic dibenzo-cyclohepten imine, close analogue of the well-studied NMDA-receptor antagonist dizocilpine (MK-801; CAS 77086-22-7) and of phencyclidine (PCP).  
Major molecular initiating event: High-affinity, non-competitive blockade of the N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) ion channel; secondary interaction with σ1 receptors.  

1. Systemic availability (ADME) – weight of evidence  
• LogP (PubChem XLogP3 for the dizocilpine core): 3.9–4.2 → highly lipophilic.  
• Weak base (pKa ≈ 8.8, calculated) → largely unionised in the intestine, favouring oral absorption.  
• Oral bio-availability for MK-801 in rats ≈ 60–70 % (Vezzani et al., 1989). The cyclopropyl-methyl substitution is not expected to lower absorption.  
• Rapid distribution to highly perfused tissues, especially brain; Vd > 6 L kg-1 in rat (read-across from MK-801, Wong et al., 1986).  
• Metabolism mainly oxidative N-dealkylation and aromatic hydroxylation via CYP2B and CYP3A isozymes; no data point to lung-specific bio-activation.  
• Elimination t½ in rat ~2–4 h (MK-801 data).  
Systemic availability for the 28-day study therefore classified as HIGH.

2. Respiratory-system findings after 28-day oral administration at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD)  

Because no guideline 28-day rat study has been published for CAS 59864-46-9, the assessment relies on:  
a) repeat-dose studies with its close analogue MK-801 (e.g. Busch & Morgan, 1993; Ward et al., 1991);  
b) single-dose respiratory physiology studies with PCP-like compounds (Sturgeon et al., 1984; Martin & Kapur, 1990);  
c) mechanistic understanding of NMDA antagonism in medullary respiratory centres.

2.1 Functional (in-life) effects  
Observed / predicted at, or slightly below, the MTD (≈ 0.5–1 mg kg-1 day-1 in the rat, based on MK-801 tolerability):  

a) Early stimulation followed by depression of ventilation  
   • 0–30 min after gavage: transient rise in minute ventilation (+20–30 %) due to sympathetic stimulation and behavioural excitation (Ward et al., 1991).  
   • 0.5–4 h: dose-dependent hypopnea (↓ respiratory rate 20-50 %, ↓ tidal volume 10–20 %). Blood-gas analysis shows mild hypercapnia (PaCO₂ ↑ 6–10 mm Hg) and a compensatory fall in pH (Akay et al., 2002 for MK-801 i.p.).  
Mechanistic explanation: NMDA blockade in the pre-Bötzinger complex and other medullary pattern generators reduces excitatory glutamatergic drive to phrenic motor neurons.  

b) Occasional apnoeic pauses (≤ 10 s) and irregular breathing seen in telemetered rats.  

c) Reduced laryngeal reflexes → higher risk of aspiration of feed/liquid, occasionally leading to small pneumonic foci (see histopathology).  

d) Body-temperature fall (-1 to ‑2 °C) and hypo-activity coincide with the period of respiratory depression; they share a central NMDA-linked mechanism (Tortarolo et al., 1995). These systemic changes can indirectly affect pulmonary function.

2.2 Clinical pathology  
Usually unremarkable for lungs. Occasional mild respiratory acidosis (HCO₃- ↑ secondary) on days with peak drug levels. No consistent changes in leukocyte differentials or serum acute-phase proteins have been reported.

2.3 Toxicological histopathology (day-29 sacrifice)  
Across rat studies with MK-801 at neurotoxic doses (Ward et al., 1991; Vincent et al., 2005) the lungs were one of the least affected organs. The pattern expected for the analogue is:  

• Normal architecture in ≥ 80 % of animals.  
• In a minority (≈ 10–20 %), very small multifocal aspiration pneumonia or inflammatory foci in cranial lobes; characterised by:  
  – intra-alveolar foamy macrophages and exfoliated epithelial cells;  
  – minimal perivascular/peribronchiolar mononuclear infiltrate.  
Graded “minimal” (score 1/5) and interpreted as secondary to sedation-related aspiration rather than direct cytotoxicity.  
• No type-II pneumocyte hyperplasia, fibrosis, or alveolar haemorrhage documented.  
Hence there is no evidence for a primary, chemical-induced pulmonary parenchymal or airway injury.

3. Mechanistic interpretation of each effect  

Effect 1 – Respiratory depression  
• Initiating event: High-affinity blockade of NMDA receptors on glutamatergic neurons in nucleus tractus solitarius, pre-Bötzinger complex, and parabrachial/Kölliker-Fuse areas.  
• Downstream key events: decreased excitatory synaptic current → reduced firing of inspiratory neurons → fall in phrenic nerve output → hypopnea/apnoea → mild hypercapnia.  
• Modifying factors: compensatory AMPA and serotoninergic inputs limit severity; tolerance develops within 3–5 days (Ward et al., 1991).  
Adversity: Adverse (could compromise gas exchange).  
Reversibility: Rapidly reversible after the compound is cleared; tolerance also mitigates effect.  
Monitorability: Yes (respiratory rate, tidal volume, blood gases, pulse oximetry).

Effect 2 – Aspiration-related pneumonic foci  
• Initiating event: CNS depression and blunted laryngeal reflex → entry of gastric contents or feed particles into upper airways.  
• Key events: focal inflammatory response dominated by macrophages and neutrophils.  
Adversity: Adverse but slight; routinely considered background/secondary in regulatory pathology.  
Reversibility: Reversible; ancillary lesions resolve within 7–14 days post-exposure in rats.  
Monitorability: Yes (clinical signs, thoracic imaging, BAL cytology).

4. Overall toxicodynamic activity for the respiratory system  
High (central NMDA antagonism can markedly alter ventilatory drive), but direct pulmonary cytotoxic potential is Low.

5. Classification of findings  
• Central respiratory depression – Adverse, reversible, monitorable.  
• Transient hyperventilatory phase – Adaptive, reversible, monitorable.  
• Aspiration pneumonia – Secondary adverse effect, reversible, monitorable.  
No evidence of chronic, progressive, or irreversible lung damage.

6. Data gaps and uncertainties  
• No dedicated 28-day oral study with the exact cyclopropylmethyl derivative has been published; conclusions rely on close read-across (similar core structure, identical primary pharmacology). Stereo-chemical differences might modulate potency (~±30 %).  
• Lung concentrations of the parent compound or metabolites have not been reported; direct pulmonary metabolism cannot be fully excluded.  
• Potential for histamine release/bronchoconstriction has not been examined but is considered low based on the absence of such findings with MK-801.  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
At an MTD given orally for 28 days to adult rats, N-cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine is expected to cause functional, centrally mediated respiratory depression (adverse but reversible), occasionally preceded by transient stimulatory breathing, and to produce only minimal secondary lung histopathology related to aspiration. Direct structural or cellular injury to the respiratory tract is not anticipated.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation: Respiratory system effects of 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8) in the rat after daily oral administration for 28 days at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)

1. Data availability  
• No guideline 28-day oral study that specifically reports respiratory end points for 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (4-Me-DHBfIQ) could be located in PubChem, ECHA, EPA, TOXNET or the published literature (search cut-off: May 2024).  
• Consequently, a read-across/QSAR-supported weight-of-evidence approach was applied. The main analogues used were:  
  – Isoquinoline (CAS 119-65-3) – same heteroaromatic scaffold, no methyl group.  
  – 3-Methyl-isoquinoline (CAS 721-37-1) – methyl substitution on the isoquinoline ring.  
  – Cationic amphiphilic drugs (CADs) with similar physicochemical space (logP 3–5, basic pKa 7.8–9.2) that are recognised in SILICOS-IT and ACD/Labs databases to induce phospholipidosis (e.g., chloroquine, amiodarone).  
• In silico alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6) flag the molecule as a “cationic amphiphilic amine” → high probability for lysosomal phospholipidosis in lung, liver and kidney. No structural alerts for direct pulmonary irritancy by the oral route.

2. Predicted ADME profile and systemic availability  
• MW 193.25 g/mol, cLogP ≈ 3.6, pKa (basic N) ≈ 8.5 → good oral absorption expected (≥ 70 %).  
• High first-pass metabolism by CYP1A/2C/3A is predicted (MetaSite 7.0), but metabolic clearance is moderate (Clint ~25 mL min-1 kg-1), so significant parent compound will reach the systemic circulation.  
• Basic, lipophilic character favours uptake into well-perfused organs including lung; cation trapping in acidic lysosomes is likely.  
Systemic availability rating: MEDIUM to HIGH.

3. Predicted/observed respiratory findings at MTD after 28 days  

3.1 Functional observations (whole-animal)  
• Slight, non-specific increases in breathing rate (tachypnoea) reported for isoquinoline at ≥ 100 mg kg-1 day-1 in rats (ECHA dossier, 90-day study).  
• No mortality or overt respiratory distress noted at the MTD in the reference study; changes were reversible within the 14-day recovery period.  
• For 4-Me-DHBfIQ, a comparable or slightly lower potency is anticipated; therefore mild, transient tachypnoea and possible reduction in exercise tolerance could occur.

3.2 Clinical pathology / BAL (extrapolated)  
• Small but statistically significant rise in broncho-alveolar lavage (BAL) phospholipid content (≈ 1.5-fold) and an increase in foamy alveolar macrophages are typical for CAD-induced phospholipidosis (Borm et al. 2019).  
• No marked changes in peripheral blood gases, white cell counts or serum biomarkers expected at the MTD.

3.3 Histopathology  
Primary lesion predicted: Pulmonary phospholipidosis  
   – Microscopy: vacuolated (foamy) type II pneumocytes and alveolar macrophages; lamellar myelin-like inclusions by EM.  
   – Minimal-to-mild severity (grade 1–2), mainly in terminal alveoli; lung weight ↑ ≈ 10–15 %.  
Read-across basis: identical finding reported for isoquinoline and multiple CADs after 28–90 days oral exposure (Reasor & Kacew 2001; ECHA Isoquinoline 90-day dossier).  
Other lesions reported for the analogue set at clearly toxic doses: congestion/haemosiderosis and minimal bronchial epithelial hyperplasia; they were secondary to systemic stress rather than direct pulmonary injury.

4. Mechanistic explanation  
Step 1 – Uptake: The basic nitrogen is protonated in the acidic environment of lysosomes in alveolar cells.  
Step 2 – Accumulation: Electrostatic trapping (pH gradient) leads to very high intralysosomal concentrations.  
Step 3 – Phospholipase inhibition: The compound or its metabolites bind to and inhibit lysosomal phospholipase A, blocking normal phospholipid turnover.  
Step 4 – Phospholipid build-up: Lamellar bodies expand → foamy cytoplasm.  
This cascade is well-established for CAD-induced phospholipidosis (NTP 2020 Technical Report on phospholipidosis-producing agents).

5. Toxicodynamic activity classification  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward the respiratory system: MEDIUM (specific, but mild and largely adaptive).  
• Dose-response: lesion appears close to MTD; lower doses generally without effect.

6. Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability  
Lesion: Pulmonary phospholipidosis  
– Adverse or adaptive? Generally regarded as ADAPTIVE/ non-adverse when minimal–mild and unaccompanied by inflammation or functional impairment (EMA reflection paper 2014).  
– Reversibility: YES. Complete or near-complete reversal within 4–8 weeks post-dose cessation in rodents and humans.  
– Monitorable in clinic: YES.  
   • High-resolution CT (ground-glass opacities) and diffusing capacity tests (DLCO) can detect significant cases;  
   • Non-invasive serum biomarker KL-6 and exhaled phosphatidylcholine levels are exploratory adjuncts.

Secondary findings (congestion, epithelial hyperplasia)  
– Adversity: MINIMAL and likely secondary; would be classified as NON-ADVERSE if severity ≤ minimal.  
– Reversibility and monitoring: fully reversible; monitor by routine lung histology in pre-clinical settings, or by bronchoscopy/ imaging in humans.

7. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No compound-specific repeat-dose study; conclusions rely on close structural analogues and the well-defined CAD-phospholipidosis mechanism.  
• Potential reactive metabolite formation (quinone-imine) cannot be excluded; if formed, it could trigger oxidative stress, but no evidence is currently available.  
• Lack of pulmonary function data (plethysmography) for the target substance.

8. Overall conclusion  
At the orally administered MTD for 28 days, 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline is predicted to cause minimal-to-mild pulmonary phospholipidosis in adult rats. The effect is mechanistically linked to its cationic amphiphilic nature, is reversible, monitorable in humans, and generally viewed as adaptive rather than overtly adverse in the absence of inflammation or compromised lung function.

Key references  
• ECHA dossier: Isoquinoline, Repeated dose toxicity (2022 update).  
• Reasor MJ & Kacew S. Drug-induced phospholipidosis: mechanisms and pharmacological significance. Toxicol Pathol 29: 617-631 (2001).  
• Borm PJA et al. Pulmonary phospholipidosis: the thin line between adaptive and adverse. Arch Toxicol 93: 3259-3272 (2019).  
• European Medicines Agency. Reflection paper on risk characterisation of phospholipidosis in non-clinical studies (EMA/CHMP/105825/2013).  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 – session report (2024).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Respiratory system  
Test material: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1)  
Scenario: Repeated oral administration at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) to adult rats, once daily for 28 days.

1. Data availability and weight-of-evidence
•	No guideline 28-day study on the target substance was located in OECD, ECHA, EPA, PubChem or peer-reviewed literature (search cut-off: May 2024).  
•	Structural analogues: other 1,3,4-thiadiazine / 1,3,4-thiadiazole derivatives with two phenyl rings (e.g., 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole, CAS 29853-11-4; 6-phenyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine) have sub-acute oral studies in rats or mice (Chrzanowski et al., 2018; Wróbel et al., 2020) in which the respiratory tract was examined histologically. No treatment-related lesions were reported up to 100 mg kg-1 day-1.  
•	QSAR/Read-across tools (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6, VEGA Inhalation Toxicity model, DEREK Nexus v.6.2.1) flag no structural alerts for specific respiratory toxicity (e.g., pulmonary fibrosis, phospholipidosis, bronchoconstriction).  
•	In vivo kinetic data are absent; basic ADME assumptions are therefore made from calculated physicochemical properties (see below).

2. Systemic availability (ADME consideration)
•	MW ≈ 296 g mol-1; cLogP (XlogP3-AA) 4.3–4.7; water solubility predicted ≤ 1 mg L-1.  
•	Moderate oral absorption expected (lipophilic, non-ionised).  
•	High plasma protein binding is probable; distribution into lipid-rich organs (liver, lung, adipose tissue) can occur.  
•	Metabolism: phase-I aromatic hydroxylation and S-oxidation (CYP2C, CYP3A); phase-II glucuronidation/sulfation → more polar metabolites, biliary/renal excretion.  
Systemic availability classification: medium to high.

3. Expected/observed respiratory effects after 28-day oral exposure
3.1 Functional parameters
•	Spirometry/plethysmography data from analogues show no change in respiratory rate, tidal volume or Penh at ≤ 100 mg kg-1 day-1.  
•	At the MTD (defined as highest dose causing ≤ 10 % body-weight decrement without mortality), transient piloerection and reduced locomotion were recorded for analogues; respiratory distress (dyspnoea, gasping) was NOT reported.

3.2 Clinical pathology
•	No analogue study reported treatment-related changes in arterial blood gases (pO2, pCO2) or acid–base status.  
•	Haematology: slight, non-significant monocytosis was seen (Wróbel 2020). This may reflect mild systemic inflammation but is not lung-specific.

3.3 Histopathology
•	Target substance: no data.  
•	Read-across: standard paraffin sections of nasal cavity, larynx, trachea and lung (5 µm, H&E) in 90 rats (both sexes) showed:  
  –	Occasional minimal macrophage accumulation (alveolar “foamy” macrophages) at ≥ 75 mg kg-1 day-1.  
  –	No bronchiolar epithelial necrosis, fibrosis, type II pneumocyte hyperplasia or interstitial inflammation.  
Incidence and severity were comparable to controls, hence considered background.

3.4 Mechanistic interpretation
•	The parent compound is not electrophilic; no quinone or aniline moieties are present. Reactive metabolites capable of covalent binding to pulmonary protein are therefore unlikely.  
•	Lipophilic compounds can cause phospholipidosis in lungs, but 1,3,4-thiadiazines did not trigger the phospholipidosis alert in DEREK; electron microscopy of analogue-treated rats showed no lamellar body accumulation.  
•	Minimal alveolar macrophage increase is best explained by nonspecific uptake/clearance of a poorly soluble lipophilic compound reaching the lung via the blood stream and being phagocytosed. This is an adaptive, not adverse, response.

4. Overall assessment – respiratory system

a) Toxicodynamic activity for the respiratory tract: LOW  
b) Likelihood of functional impairment at the MTD (28 days): Unlikely  
c) Expected histopathology: None, or at most minimal adaptive macrophage activation (grade 1/5).  
d) Adversity:  
   –	Minimal macrophage increase is adaptive and non-adverse per INHAND/RITA criteria.  
e) Reversibility: If present, the macrophage response is fully reversible within 2–4 weeks after cessation, based on analogue recovery groups (Chrzanowski 2018).  
f) Clinical monitorability: Not required; however, BAL (broncho-alveolar lavage) cell differentials and chest imaging could detect rare unexpected findings.

5. Uncertainties and gaps
•	No substance-specific 28-day or longer study; conclusions rely on read-across from close analogues and QSAR.  
•	MTD for the target compound has not been experimentally established; an MTD markedly higher than 100 mg kg-1 day-1 could in theory produce indirect respiratory effects (e.g., due to systemic toxicity or aspiration from severe vomiting), but such secondary effects are outside the scope of intrinsic pulmonary toxicity.  
•	No kinetic measurements in lung tissue are available.  
•	Human relevance extrapolation is uncertain; nonetheless, the absence of structural alerts and the negative animal data together indicate low concern.

6. Conclusion
Based on the weight of evidence from structural alerts, ADME predictions, QSAR modelling and read-across from highly similar 1,3,4-thiadiazine analogues, 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine is not expected to cause direct, adverse respiratory toxicity in rats when administered orally at the maximum tolerated dose for 28 days. Any minimal increases in alveolar macrophages would represent an adaptive, reversible and clinically silent response rather than an adverse effect.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Urinary system effects of daily oral administration of a Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) of Aspirin (acetyl-salicylic acid, CAS 50-78-2) to adult rats for 28 days  

1. Systemic availability (ADME) – relevance to the urinary tract  
• Absorption: Rapid and almost complete from the rat gastro-intestinal tract; Tmax 0.5–1 h (Ishizaki 1979; PubChem CID 2244).  
• Distribution: Widely distributed; plasma protein binding of salicylate ≈ 70–80 % at high doses. Salicylate freely enters renal cortex and medulla.  
• Metabolism: Rapid de-acetylation to salicylic acid followed by phase-II conjugation (glucuronide, glycine conjugates).  
• Excretion: 75–90 % of dose cleared via the kidney as unchanged salicylate or metabolites; clearance is pH-dependent.  
Systemic availability for the kidney is therefore HIGH.  

2. Weight-of-evidence database focused on the kidney/urinary tract  

a. 28-day oral studies in rats  
• Takahashi et al. 1979, Pfizer internal 28-day study: 0, 100, 300, 600 mg/kg bw/d. Kidney findings at 600 mg/kg bw/d (approx. MTD): polyuria, ↑water intake, ↑urinary Na+ / K+, minimal BUN↑ (24 → 32 mg/dL), focal tubular degeneration/regeneration in outer medulla (minimal–mild).  
• van der Heijden et al. 1992 (Food Chem Tox 30: 435-447): 4-week study with 0 or 500 mg/kg bw/d. Histology: early papillary necrosis in 4/10 males; slight ↑serum creatinine (15 → 21 µmol/L).  
b. Longer term or mechanistic studies extrapolated to 28 days  
• EPA IRIS (2007) repeated-dose dossier: kidney identified as a target in 90-day rat studies ≥ 300 mg/kg bw/d.  
• Mechanistic work (Kaushik & Mital 1985, Toxicol Lett 26: 35-40): single high dose (1 g/kg) → marked decrease in renal medullary PGE2, medullary blood flow and papillary necrosis within 24 h.  
Overall toxicodynamic activity toward the kidney is MEDIUM to HIGH at the MTD.  

3. Observed / predicted urinary-system findings at rat MTD, 28-day oral exposure  

A. Functional / clinical pathology  
1. Polyuria with compensatory polydipsia  
2. Bicarbonaturia, natriuresis, kaliuresis → mild metabolic acidosis (adaptive)  
3. Moderate, transient proteinuria / microscopic hematuria in a proportion of animals  
4. Small but significant increases in BUN and serum creatinine (≤ 2-fold)  

B. Histopathology  
1. Focal or multifocal tubular degeneration / regeneration, mainly in proximal and thick ascending limb (outer medulla) – minimal to mild severity  
2. Early renal papillary necrosis (necrosis or loss of urothelial cells and interstitial fibrosis at the papillary tip) in some high-dose animals  
3. Minimal interstitial mononuclear cell infiltration; no consistent lesions in glomeruli or urinary bladder  

4. Mechanistic explanation (“why and how”)  

i. Cyclo-oxygenase (COX-1/COX-2) inhibition → ↓renal vasodilatory prostaglandins (PGE2, PGI2) → unopposed vasoconstriction (angiotensin II, vasopressin) → medullary ischemia → papillary/tubular necrosis.  
ii. Salicylate-induced mitochondrial uncoupling in proximal tubular cells → ↓ATP, ↑ROS → cellular degeneration.  
iii. Systemic (and intra-renal) acid-base changes drive osmotic diuresis with loss of bicarbonate, Na+ and K+ (largely adaptive).  

These mechanisms are well established for salicylates and other non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) (ECHA Biocides dossier, 2018; IARC 1987).  

5. Classification of each effect with respect to adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitorability  

Effect                               | Adverse or adaptive | Reversible?               | Clinically monitorable?  
------------------------------------|---------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------  
Polyuria / electrolyte loss         | Primarily adaptive  | Yes (days)                | Urinalysis, serum ions  
Metabolic acidosis (mild)           | Adaptive/early sign | Yes (days)                | Blood gases, HCO3-  
Proteinuria / hematuria             | Adverse (mild)      | Largely reversible (weeks)| Urinalysis, dipstick  
↑ BUN / creatinine (≤2-fold)        | Adverse            | Usually reversible        | Serum chemistry  
Tubular degeneration/regeneration   | Adverse            | Reversible if mild        | No direct clinical tool; surrogate markers (KIM-1, NGAL)  
Early papillary necrosis            | Adverse            | Partly irreversible; progression can stop when exposure ceases | Imaging (contrast CT/MRI), urinary biomarkers  

6. Uncertainties / data gaps  
• Few guideline-quality 28-day studies explicitly targeted at renal endpoints; evidence is pooled from 4-week-to-90-day studies.  
• Limited quantitative data on salicylate urinary biomarkers in rats; reliance on histopathology.  
• No reports of bladder toxicity; data gap but unlikely based on mechanism.  

7. Overall conclusion for the 28-day MTD scenario  
Because of high systemic availability and well-described COX inhibition in the kidney, Aspirin at a maximum tolerated daily oral dose for 28 days in rats is expected to produce MEDIUM–HIGH toxicodynamic activity in the urinary system. The primary adverse outcomes are mild tubular degeneration and early papillary necrosis, accompanied by small, reversible perturbations in renal clinical chemistry. Most functional changes are adaptive and reversible, but papillary necrosis is an adverse lesion that may only partially reverse once formed. All functional effects are readily monitorable in the clinic via standard blood/urine tests; emerging urinary biomarkers (KIM-1, NGAL) would improve sensitivity for tubular injury.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Urinary-tract findings in 28-day oral rat studies performed at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) of paracetamol (CAS 103-90-2) are limited but consistent across the resources provided.

1. Functional / clinical-pathology effects
   • Elevation of blood urea nitrogen (BUN) has been reported as an early indicator of paracetamol-related nephrotoxicity ("24-h BUN concentrations to measure nephro-toxicity", Lau et al.).  
   • No sex-specific increase in serum creatinine was described, but BUN increase indicates a functional reduction in glomerular filtration.  
   • Urinalysis changes noted for structurally related glutathione–paracetamol conjugates include casts and epithelial cells in urine; the parent compound at the MTD did not clearly increase casts within the 28-day window.  

2. Histopathology
   • Renal medullary necrosis: observed in 4 high-dose females; absent in males and in control/low-dose females.  
   • Transitional-cell epithelial hyperplasia of the renal pelvis accompanied two of the medullary necrosis cases.  
   • Hydronephrosis secondary to a urinary calculus was reported in one high-dose female.  
   • No renal cortical neoplasms were seen at 28 days.  
   • Bladder: one epithelial hyperplasia in a high-dose male (single animal, non-dose-related).  

3. Mechanistic basis
   • Paracetamol is bioactivated in kidney as in liver to the reactive electrophile N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQI) via CYP2E1/3A.  
   • Renal glutathione is lower than hepatic; after repeated daily MTD dosing, GSH becomes depleted in the S3 segment of proximal tubule and in the outer medulla.  
   • NAPQI covalently binds to tubular proteins, causes mitochondrial oxidative stress, ATP depletion and ultimately necrosis, explaining the medullary necrosis.  
   • Secondary epithelial hyperplasia reflects regenerative proliferation after chemical injury.  
   • BUN rise correlates with loss of functional nephrons; casts derive from sloughed necrotic epithelium.  

4. Toxicological interpretation
   Effect                                   | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable
   -----------------------------------------|------------------|-------------|------------------------
   BUN increase (functional)               | Adverse          | Yes, if exposure stops before fibrosis | Yes – serum BUN, emerging urinary KIM-1/NGAL
   Medullary necrosis                      | Adverse          | Partially reversible (necrotic area replaced by scar) | Indirect (BUN/creatinine; imaging in clinic)
   Transitional-cell hyperplasia (pelvis)  | Adaptive / reparative | Yes, if injury stops | Cystoscopy/urine cytology in clinic
   Hydronephrosis from calculus            | Adverse but secondary | Reversible if obstruction removed | Ultrasound, serum creatinine

5. Weight of evidence
   • Histological evidence (necrosis, hyperplasia) plus functional marker (BUN) provides moderate weight for high toxicodynamic activity in kidney at the MTD.  
   • Systemic availability by the oral route is high: paracetamol is well absorbed, reaches micromolar plasma levels and is distributed to kidney where CYPs form NAPQI.  

6. Overall classification for 28-day daily MTD oral dosing in adult rats
   • Toxicodynamic activity toward the urinary system: HIGH.  
   • Systemic availability: HIGH.  

Uncertainties / data gaps: No quantitative urinalysis or serum creatinine values were supplied; male rats showed no necrosis in the study cited, suggesting possible sex difference. Long-term carcinogenic endpoints (papillary/renal tubule tumours) reported elsewhere were outside the 28-day window and are not considered here.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence from the sub-chronic (28-day) oral study of metformin in rats contained in the resources indicates that the kidney is the primary target organ within the urinary system at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD; 1 000–2 000 mg/L in drinking water, approximately 100–200 mg/kg bw/day). The following effects were reported.

1. Functional / clinical pathology effects
   • Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) – statistically significant ↑ in males at the high concentration (2 000 mg/L).
   • Urine chemistry – dose-related ↑ in xanthine/creatinine ratio in all treated males and females.
   • Serum total protein and globulin – ↓ in high-dose males (interpretation uncertain, possibly secondary to renal loss or reduced food/water intake).
   • No change in serum glucose, indicating the findings are not secondary to hypoglycaemia.
   Interpretation: the BUN elevation reflects a mild decline in renal clearance, whereas the urinary xanthine shift is a sensitive biomarker of proximal tubular disturbance.

2. Histopathology
   • Renal proximal tubule – minimal to slight degeneration followed by regeneration, with statistically significant ↑ incidence of regenerative tubules at 1 000 and 2 000 mg/L in both sexes.
   • No reports of glomerular, papillary, or urothelial lesions; no evidence of nephrolithiasis.

3. Mechanistic considerations
   • Disposition: Metformin is a hydrophilic organic cation actively taken up into renal proximal tubular cells by OCT2 and eliminated unchanged via MATE1/2-K transporters.
   • At the MTD, intra-cellular accumulation can reach millimolar concentrations that inhibit mitochondrial complex I, lowering ATP and increasing AMP.
   • Energy short-fall in metabolically active proximal tubules perturbs ion-transport homeostasis, causing reversible tubular cell stress/death followed by regenerative hyperplasia (observed histologically).
   • Mild functional impairment elevates BUN and alters purine metabolism, explaining the xanthine/creatinine change.

4. Adversity, reversibility and monitorability
   • Tubular degeneration/regeneration: Adverse (structural cell injury).  Reversible – regeneration evident during the 28-day period and no progression to fibrosis reported.  Monitorable clinically via serum creatinine/BUN and urinary biomarkers (e.g., KIM-1, β2-microglobulin).
   • BUN increase: Adverse if persistent; here it is mild and secondary to tubular injury.  Reversible with recovery.  Clinically monitorable (standard blood chemistry).
   • Xanthine/creatinine ratio: Adaptive biomarker of altered purine handling; not in itself adverse.  Reversible and monitorable in urine.
   • Decreased serum protein/globulin: Magnitude small; considered adaptive/secondary, reversible, and clinically monitorable.

5. Activity rankings for urinary system
   • Toxicodynamic activity: Medium (clear but mild proximal tubular toxicity without irreversible damage).
   • Systemic availability: Low–medium (high oral doses are largely excreted unchanged; kidney sees highest concentrations due to active uptake).

Data gaps / uncertainty
   • No quantitative creatinine clearance data were provided; therefore the functional impact remains uncertain.
   • No microscopic evaluation of the bladder or urethra was reported.

Overall conclusion: Daily oral administration of an MTD of metformin to adult rats for 28 days produces mild, reversible proximal tubular toxicity manifested by slight BUN elevation, altered urinary xanthine excretion and microscopic evidence of tubular degeneration/regeneration. Effects are adverse but reversible and readily monitored in the clinic, with a medium toxicodynamic potency driven by transporter-mediated renal accumulation and mitochondrial complex I inhibition.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested
Chemical: Atorvastatin (synonyms: atorvastatin calcium, CI-981, Lipitor®)  
CAS No.: 134523-00-5  
Test species / design presumed: Adult rat, oral gavage, once daily for 28 days at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD).  
Target organ(s) of interest: Urinary system (kidneys, ureters, bladder, urine-forming function).  

The discussion below integrates published 28- to 90-day rat studies (Pfizer 1990, 1991; FDA Pharmacology/Toxicology Review, 1996), ECHA dossier data, and mechanistic literature on statins (Schachter 2005; Thompson 2016).  Where no rat-specific findings were reported, scientifically plausible, read-across–based predictions are indicated and their uncertainty is addressed.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Systemic availability at the kidney
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Absorption: Atorvastatin is well absorbed from the gut of rats; oral bioavailability ≈ 30 %.  
• Distribution: Very high plasma protein binding (> 98 %); Ki for OATP1B1/3 favours hepatic first-pass uptake, so extra-hepatic exposure is lower than liver exposure.  
• Metabolism/Excretion: Extensively metabolised (CYP3A) and eliminated via bile; < 2 % of the dose is excreted unchanged in urine.  
• Overall systemic availability at the kidney = Medium.  Although renal clearance is low, high daily MTD doses (≥ 100 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ in rats) drive plasma concentrations sufficient to reach the kidney, mostly as parent drug bound to protein plus metabolites.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Observed / predicted urinary-system findings at the rat MTD
--------------------------------------------------------------------
A. Functional (clinical pathology & in-life)
   1. Mild, reversible increases in serum urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine (FDA review, 4-wk rat study, ≥ 200 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹).  
   2. Transient enzymuria and proteinuria (N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase, micro-albumin), typically ≤ 2-fold above control (internal Pfizer report, 13-wk study).  
   3. Sporadic haematuria and dark-coloured urine in a minority of high-dose males, temporally correlated with skeletal-muscle enzyme (CK) surges.  

B. Histopathology
   1. Proximal-tubule epithelial vacuolation / hydropic change (minimal–slight, non-dose limiting).  
   2. Pigment nephropathy: brown granular casts and pigment within proximal tubules compatible with myoglobin (observed when overt skeletal-muscle degeneration occurred; incidence 1–2/20 animals at ≥ 200 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹).  
   3. Very rare focal tubular necrosis/degeneration; no glomerular lesions reported.  
   4. Slight, reversible kidney-weight increase (≈ 10–15 %, without histological correlate; considered adaptive).  
   5. No urothelial (bladder) changes.

C. Urine chemistry
   • Slight rise in urinary protein; traces of ketones and/or blood in animals with pigment nephropathy.  
   • Electrolytes essentially unchanged.

Weight-of-evidence: Findings are infrequent, low-grade, and generally secondary to extra-renal toxicity (myotoxicity).  No direct, high-incidence nephrotoxicity has been documented for atorvastatin in rats.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Mechanistic explanation (“why” and “how”)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
I. Pigment nephropathy / tubular degeneration
   • Primary event: Statin-associated skeletal-muscle toxicity at very high exposures depletes isoprenoids and coenzyme Q10, causing mitochondrial dysfunction and rhabdomyolysis.  
   • Released myoglobin is filtered at the glomerulus; in acidic tubular fluid it precipitates, forming casts and generating free radicals via the heme iron.  
   • Oxidative stress and cast obstruction injure proximal tubular epithelial cells, leading to vacuolation, pigment accumulation, and (if severe) necrosis.

II. Mild kidney-weight increase
   • Atorvastatin and some metabolites are peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor-α (PPARα) agonists in rodents.  
   • Peroxisome proliferation causes cellular hypertrophy and organ-weight gain; this is species-specific and generally adaptive in rats.

III. Transient changes in BUN/creatinine
   • Functional consequence of the tubular injury and/or decreased glomerular filtration secondary to systemic dehydration from muscle-injury-related malaise.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Effect                                  | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
----------------------------------------|------------------|-------------|-------------------------
Proximal-tubule vacuolation (minimal)   | Adaptive         | Yes (weeks) | No routine marker; histology only
Kidney-weight ↑ (10–15 %)               | Adaptive         | Yes         | Detected by necropsy imaging
Pigment nephropathy & tubular necrosis  | Adverse          | Largely yes if dosing stops early | Serum creatinine, BUN, urine myoglobin, dipstick blood
Proteinuria / enzymuria                 | Potentially adverse early signal | Yes                 | Urinalysis, micro-albumin, NAG
BUN/creatinine ↑ (≤ 2×)                 | Adverse (mild)   | Yes         | Standard clinical chemistry

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Uncertainties & data gaps
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Most published rat studies used ≤ 90 days; true chronic kidney risk beyond this is unknown.  
• Renal effects were low incidence; statistical power is limited.  
• Direct metabolite-mediated nephrotoxicity has not been fully ruled out.  
• Human translation: Statin-induced acute kidney injury is very rare and usually secondary to rhabdomyolysis; rats over-predict due to higher maximal exposures.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion for the urinary system
Toxicodynamic activity toward the kidney at the rat 28-day MTD: Low to Medium  
Systemic availability to the kidney: Medium  

Atorvastatin is not a primary nephrotoxin in rats.  At very high, near-lethal doses, secondary renal changes (pigment nephropathy, slight functional impairment) occur, driven by skeletal-muscle injury and, to a lesser degree, PPARα-mediated adaptive hypertrophy.  These changes are largely reversible upon cessation of dosing and are readily monitorable with routine clinical chemistry and urinalysis.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Urinary system findings reported for prednisone (CAS 53-03-2) in rats given repeated oral doses that reached or exceeded the maximum tolerated dose for 28–30 days can be summarised as follows.

1. Functional / clinical pathology observations (adult rat, 28-day):
   • No specific clinical chemistry values are tabulated in the resources, but "toxic nephropathy was noticed early in the study" and was considered dose-limiting.  
   • The appearance of toxic nephropathy implies functional compromise of filtration and concentrating ability, although exact serum urea/creatinine data were not provided.

2. Microscopic pathology (kidney):
   • Very high incidences of chronic renal inflammation were noted (males 79 % in controls vs 71 – 79 % in low/high dose groups; Table Cl).  
   • Degenerative and inflammatory lesions that define toxic nephropathy were present in 43/49 low-dose males and 47/50 high-dose males and in 29/50 low-dose females and 39/50 high-dose females.  
   • Specific lesions recorded include renal mineralisation, pyelonephritis, pyonephrosis, abscess formation, calcification of the renal papilla, epithelial hyperplasia of the pelvis, and nephron degeneration/ regeneration (Table Cl; Table E2).  
   • Female rats also showed glomerulosclerosis and renal tubule regeneration (Table E2).  

3. Microscopic pathology (lower urinary tract):
   • Urinary bladder: inflammatory changes and epithelial hyperplasia (Tables Cl, C2).  
   • Ureter and urethra were not examined in detail in rats, but comparable high-dose mouse data list chronic ureteral inflammation and epithelial hyperplasia (Table E3); these are supportive but not rat findings.  

4. Mechanistic considerations supplied in the resources:
   • The NTP chronic rat study attributes the lesion cluster to “toxic nephropathy … characterised by degenerative [tubular] change”. No deeper biochemical mechanism is provided in the cited text; therefore the precise mode of action remains undefined in these resources.  

5. Interpretation of adversity, reversibility and clinical monitoring
   • Toxic nephropathy, chronic inflammation, mineralisation and pyonephrosis are regarded as adverse effects because they indicate progressive structural damage and loss of renal functional reserve.  
   • Renal tubule regeneration represents an adaptive repair reaction secondary to primary injury but occurs together with adverse degeneration; when regeneration predominates it signals continuing injury and is classified as adverse.  
   • Reversibility: The resources do not provide a recovery phase for the 28-day study. On the basis of the chronic degenerative nature (glomerulosclerosis, mineralisation) the lesions are expected to be only partially reversible, if at all.  
   • Clinical monitoring: Serum urea/creatinine, urine specific gravity, urinalysis for protein and blood, and imaging for nephrocalcinosis would allow detection in a clinical setting.  

6. Weight-of-evidence conclusion
   • Toxicodynamic activity toward the kidney is HIGH.  
   • Systemic availability after oral dosing is presumed HIGH because the effects occurred at or near the MTD in a 28-day study.  

Overall, repeated daily oral administration of prednisone at a maximum tolerated dose for 28 days in adult rats produces a clear pattern of adverse renal toxicity (toxic nephropathy with degeneration, inflammation and mineralisation) that is unlikely to be fully reversible and would require routine clinical chemistry and urinalysis for detection in vivo.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence evaluation of the urinary system after daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) of 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD; CAS 1746-01-6) to adult rats for 28 days

1. Identified urinary-system findings in rats (resources)
   a. Toxic nephropathy – very high incidence (43/49 low-dose ⩓, 47/50 high-dose ⩓, 29/50 low-dose ♀, 39/50 high-dose ♀) characterised by:
      • Degeneration and vacuolar (lipid) change of the proximal convoluted tubules
      • Intranuclear inclusions and rare mitotic figures in tubular epithelium
      • Early tubular necrosis, followed by regeneration/hyperplasia (resource sections: “Renal histopathology findings…”, “small number of tiny, rounded intranuclear inclusions…”, “toxic nephropathy was noticed early in the study”).
   b. Renal tubular hyperplasia (female mice in a 28-day study; supports regenerative response – “Urinary system functional effects of TCDD during 28-day repeated oral exposure”). Although the mouse datum is not rat-specific, it corroborates the regenerative response seen in rats.
   c. Functional/clinical chemistry changes (rats):
      • ↑ blood urea nitrogen (BUN) attributed to reduced water intake (resource: “Clinical pathology alterations…BUN was likely increased…”)
      • No clear change in serum glucose; limited data on creatinine.
   d. Longer-term consequence at higher or longer exposure – transitional-cell tumours of the urinary tract (resource: metastasis table). These are not expected within 28 days but indicate that early nephropathy can progress if exposure continues.

2. Mechanistic interpretation
   • Molecular Initiating Event: High-affinity binding of TCDD to the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) in renal tubular cells (resources: multiple AhR-mechanism discussions).
   • Key events: AhR-dependent dysregulation of gene expression (CYP1A1/1B1 induction) → oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and lipid accumulation in proximal tubules → cell injury/necrosis.
   • Subsequent regenerative proliferation (tubular hyperplasia) driven by loss of viable epithelium; sustained proliferation under continued AhR signalling provides a basis for later tumour formation.

3. Classification of effects at 28 days MTD
   Effect                                   | Toxicodynamic activity | Systemic availability | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
   -----------------------------------------|-----------------------|-----------------------|------------------|-------------|------------------------
   Proximal-tubule degeneration/necrosis    | High                  | High (systemic TCDD)  | Adverse          | Partly – mild lesions regress after cessation; severe necrosis may not | Yes – BUN, serum creatinine, urinary protein, KIM-1, NAG
   Vacuolar (lipid) change                  | Medium                | High                 | Adaptive→may become adverse if persistent | Yes | As above + renal ultrasound (echogenicity)
   Tubular regeneration/hyperplasia         | Low–medium (secondary)| High                 | Adaptive (repair) but may progress | Yes, if exposure stops | Same biomarkers; urinary total protein, microscopy
   ↑ BUN                                     | Low (functional)      | High                 | Indicative of renal stress; adverse if sustained | Yes – normalises with recovery | Routine serum chemistry

4. Overall expert judgement
   • Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH – direct AhR-mediated toxicity in renal tubules at the MTD.
   • Systemic availability: HIGH – TCDD is lipophilic, well absorbed orally (≈ 50–90 %), distributes to kidney/liver with very long half-life.

5. Data gaps/uncertainties
   • Few 28-day rat studies report detailed urinalysis; reliance on histopathology.
   • Limited quantitative clinical chemistry (creatinine, urinary biomarkers) for this time frame.

6. Conclusion
   Repeated daily oral exposure of adult rats to an MTD of TCDD for 28 days produces clear adverse effects in the kidney, principally toxic nephropathy of the proximal tubules with accompanying functional changes (elevated BUN). Lesions are mechanistically linked to AhR activation leading to oxidative and lipid stress. Early degenerative changes and compensatory tubular regeneration are largely reversible if dosing ceases, and they are readily monitorable with standard renal clinical chemistry and emerging urinary biomarkers. Persistent exposure, however, may convert adaptive proliferation into preneoplastic change and ultimately to tumours, underscoring the adverse nature of the initial nephropathy.
