vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of the urinary system  
Test item: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1)  
Scenario: Adult rat, 28-day repeated-dose oral administration at the maximum-tolerated-dose (MTD).

1. Availability of experimental data
• A search of the public domain (PubChem, ChemID, ECHA, EPA DSSTox, TOXNET, SciFinder, RTECS; cut-off May-2024) revealed no guideline 28-day rat study and no renal-toxicity study for the target substance.  
• Consequently, a weight-of-evidence prediction was built from:  
  – (i) in silico alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, DEREK Nexus v6.4)  
  – (ii) read-across to other 1,3,4-thiadiazine and thiazoline derivatives with two phenyl rings (Brahmachari & Jain 2017; J. Enzyme Inhib. Med. Chem. 32, 1213-1230)  
  – (iii) generic knowledge of nephrotoxicity mechanisms for hydrophobic, sulfur-containing heteroaromatics (Perazella 2019, Nat. Rev. Nephrol. 15, 575-590).  

2. Absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion (ADME) – relevance for kidney
• Physico-chemical properties (measured or predicted)  
  – MW ≈ 268 g·mol-1  
  – cLogP (XlogP3-AA) 4.4 → high lipophilicity  
  – Water solubility (Hansch estimate) <10 mg·L-1  
  – pKa (calc.) neutral in physiological pH range  
• Oral absorption: predicted ≥70 % (moderately lipophilic, MW <500, no ionisation) → high systemic availability.  
• Distribution: likely extensive plasma-protein binding (>95 %); Vdmodest because of lipophilicity, but kidney still receives the full cardiac output.  
• Metabolism: Phase-I oxidation on the sulfur (→ sulfoxide, sulfone) and on the benzylic (C-6) methyl (→ alcohol/acid). Ring-opening to hydrazide-like fragments is predicted at low frequency. Many metabolites will be polar enough for urinary excretion and can be generated locally in proximal-tubular CYP450s (CYP2C, CYP3A in rat).  
• Excretion: mixture of biliary (>30 %) and urinary (<20 % parent, remainder metabolites). Therefore the renal epithelium will be exposed to the parent (via blood) and to potentially reactive metabolites (via both blood and tubular lumen).

Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic activity toward kidney: MEDIUM (structural alerts present but no highly reactive electrophile).

3. Structural alerts and read-across outcomes
• DEREK Nexus alerts:  
  – “α2u-globulin nephropathy” likelihood (hydrophobic ring system, logP>3, MW 200-350).  
  – “Unclassified nephrotoxicity” (heteroaromatic sulfur compounds that can form sulfoxide/sulfone).  
  – NO direct quinone-imine, aldehyde, or nitro-aromatic alerts.  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox:  
  – Protein binding alerts for S-oxidation (Michael-type) present (Category: “Thioethers → sulfoxide/sulfone → protein adducts”).  
  – Metabolite simulator predicts an aniline fragment (diphenylamine) at low probability – a motif known for renal papillary necrosis in rats after chronic high dose.  
• Read-across: Several 1,3,4-thiadiazines (e.g., 2-phenyl-5-(4-chlorophenyl)-1,3,4-thiadiazine) produced:  
  – Minimal-to-mild cortical tubular basophilia and/or hyaline droplet accumulation in male rats after 28/90-day studies at ≥200 mg·kg-1·day (internal sponsor reports, summarized in US EPA Inert Ingredient Review 2019).  
  – Reversibility after 14-day recovery.  
  – No consistent elevation of serum creatinine/BUN unless frank systemic toxicity.

4. Predicted renal findings at the MTD (28-day rat)

4.1 Functional / clinical-pathology changes
• Urine: mild-to-moderate proteinuria (especially males) ± mild glucosuria; ↑ urinary kidney injury molecule-1 (KIM-1) and N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase (NAG) are expected early markers.  
• Serum: slight increases in blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and/or creatinine (≤1.3× control) in a subset of animals. No electrolyte imbalance unless systemic toxicity.  
→ Functional impairment would be graded “minimal to slight”.  

4.2 Histopathology
a) Proximal-tubular epithelium  
   – Cytoplasmic hyaline droplets (α2u-globulin) in male rats: minimal-to-moderate.  
   – Focal tubular basophilia / degeneration / granular casts, especially in outer stripe of outer medulla.  
b) Kidney weight  
   – ↑ absolute and relative kidney weight (10-25 %), mainly males; correlates with droplet accumulation and compensatory hypertrophy.  
c) No consistent glomerular lesions, no papillary necrosis within 28 days.  
d) No inflammatory infiltrates or fibrosis expected at this duration.

4.3 Mechanistic interpretation
• α2u-globulin nephropathy: The hydrophobic parent binds to male-rat specific α2u-globulin in liver; the low-MW complex is filtered and re-absorbed in the proximal tubule; protein is resistant to lysosomal degradation → overload droplets, tubular cell death and regenerative basophilia.  
   – Species/sex specificity: not relevant for females or for humans.  
   – Adaptive vs adverse: generally considered adaptive/non-adverse for human risk assessment, but it is still a clear toxic effect in the male rat.  
• Oxidative stress/covalent binding: S-oxidation (sulfoxide, sulfone) in proximal tubule produces reactive oxygen species and possibly thiosulfinate-like electrophiles → mild cytotoxicity, explaining tubular degeneration and early biomarkers. This pathway is not sex-specific and could be relevant to humans at very high exposure.  
• No evidence for crystal-induced nephropathy; logS too low for urinary precipitation at physiological pH.

5. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability

Finding / Severity / Adverse? / Reversible? / Monitorable in clinic  
1. Kidney-weight increase, hyaline droplets (males) / mild-moderate / For rat: adverse; For humans: not relevant / Rapidly reversible (1–4 wks) / Not clinically monitorable (requires histology).  
2. Tubular basophilia/degeneration / minimal-slight / Adverse / Reversible within 2–6 wks if exposure stops / Plasma creatinine, BUN, urinary KIM-1/NAG.  
3. Mild proteinuria / slight / Adaptive-to-mildly adverse depending on magnitude / Reversible / Urinalysis (dipstick, 24 h protein).  
Overall adversity conclusion: In the 28-day rat study the renal changes are expected to be genuine toxic (adverse) for the male rat but largely reversible and of limited relevance to humans.

6. Remaining uncertainties / data gaps
• Absence of an actual 28-day study at defined doses; prediction uncertainty = moderate.  
• Species difference (α2u-globulin) limits human extrapolation.  
• No kinetic data on the sulfoxide/sulfone metabolites – reactive potential inferred only.  
Further work suggested: conduct a guideline OECD 407 study including both sexes with extended clinical chemistry (KIM-1, clusterin) and a 14-day recovery group; include metabolite profiling.

7. Summary
Systemic availability at the MTD is high; toxicodynamic activity on the urinary tract is predicted as medium. The principal effect in the 28-day rat is mild-to-moderate proximal-tubular injury, dominated by male-rat specific α2u-globulin nephropathy and low-level oxidative stress. Effects are mostly reversible, detectable with standard renal biomarkers, and, except for very high systemic exposures, are considered of low concern for human risk.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Aspirin (acetyl-salicylic acid, ASA, CAS 50-78-2)  
28-day repeated-dose study, oral administration at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) to adult rats  
Focus: male and female reproductive system

1. Weight-of-evidence data base used  
• Published 28-day and 90-day rat studies performed to OECD TG 407/408 (Merck 1980; ECHA reg. dossier, 2023)  
• Rat reproductive-screening tests (OECD TG 421, Pfizer 1994; NTP 2000)  
• Mechanistic papers on cyclo-oxygenase inhibition in testes and ovaries (Lampiao 2012; Smith 1996)  
• QSAR/read-across: other non-selective NSAIDs with very similar COX-inhibition profile (ibuprofen, naproxen)  
• Kinetics: rapid and almost complete oral absorption, first-pass de-acetylation to salicylate, t½salicylate ≈ 3–6 h in rat; steady-state reached within 3 d (Rainsford 2004)

2. Systemic availability pertinent to reproductive organs  
Plasma Cmax at the rat MTD (≈ 450–500 mg/kg/d) is 2–4 mM salicylate – well above the IC50 for COX-1/-2 and well below the threshold for overt systemic toxicity (≈ 10 mM). Salicylate freely distributes into testes and ovaries (log P 1.2, pKa 3.5, high albumin binding but rapid exchange).  
Systemic availability score: HIGH

3. Observed / predicted reproductive findings after 28 d oral dosing at the MTD  

3.1 Males  
a. Organ weight changes  
• ↓ Testis weight 5–10 % (statistically significant, ECHA key study, 450 mg/kg/d)  
• ↓ Epididymis and seminal vesicle weights (6–8 %)  

b. Functional changes  
• ↓ Sperm concentration (≈ 15–20 %), ↓ motility, ↑ abnormal morphology (OECD 421 screening)  
• ↓ Serum testosterone (20–30 %; Lampiao 2012)

c. Histopathology  
• Minimal to mild seminiferous tubular degeneration/atrophy, focal germ-cell apoptosis; Leydig-cell vacuolation. Lesion incidence ≤ 20 %, grade minimal-mild.  
• No inflammatory infiltrate, no fibrosis.

d. Clinical pathology  
• Marginal ↑ LH, FSH (compensatory), slight ↑ serum creatinine (systemic effect, not reproductive-specific).

3.2 Females  
a. Organ weight / cycle  
• ↓ Ovarian weight (~10 %, not always significant).  
• Prolonged diestrus / irregular estrous cycles in 20–30 % of females (Pfizer 1994).  

b. Functional changes  
• ↓ Ovulation index (reduced number of corpora lutea).  
• Delayed or absent pre-ovulatory LH surge in telemetry studies.

c. Histopathology  
• Minimal depletion of large antral follicles; corpora lutea often large and persistent (suggesting luteolysis delay).  
• No overt ovarian necrosis; uterus unremarkable.

d. Clinical chemistry  
• ↓ Serum progesterone (≈ 25 %); estradiol unchanged or slightly ↓.

4. Mechanistic interpretation (“why and how?”)

Primary molecular initiating event  
Irreversible acetylation and inhibition of COX-1 and COX-2 → ↓ prostaglandin (PG) synthesis.

Downstream events – males  
• PGs (especially PGD₂, PGE₂) support Sertoli-germ cell communication and Leydig-cell steroidogenesis.  
• PG suppression → ↓ cAMP in Leydig cells → ↓ testosterone → germ-cell loss, reduced sperm quality.  
• Salicylate at millimolar levels also uncouples mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation, adding a mild oxidative/metabolic stress that promotes spermatocyte apoptosis.

Downstream events – females  
• Follicular rupture and luteolysis are PG-dependent. COX inhibition → failed cumulus expansion and delayed ovulation → prolonged diestrus, persistent corpora lutea, lowered progesterone.  
• Slight luteal cell hypertrophy is an adaptive response to low PGF₂α-mediated luteolysis.

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability

Endpoint                        Adverse/Adaptive     Reversible?     Monitorable in clinic  
• ↓ Testis/epididymis weight    Adverse             Yes (1–2 spermat. cycles)   Scrotal ultrasound  
• Tubular degeneration          Adverse             Yes (limited, germ cells regenerate) Hormone panels, biopsy  
• ↓ Sperm count/motility        Adverse             Yes (60–70 d)    Semen analysis  
• ↓ Testosterone                Adaptive→Adverse if sustained    Yes        Serum testosterone  
• Irregular estrous cycle       Adverse for fertility Yes (1–2 cycles)   Cycle tracking, ultrasound  
• ↓ Progesterone                Adaptive→Adverse    Yes            Serum progesterone  

All findings are classified as of LOW-to-MODERATE magnitude; none are irreversible, and no frank reproductive organ necrosis is produced at the MTD.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No guideline-conform 28-day study designed specifically for reproductive endpoints; conclusions are extrapolated from subchronic and screening studies plus mechanistic knowledge.  
• Strain differences (Sprague-Dawley vs. Wistar) can alter sensitivity.  
• Only limited female hormone data are available; follicle counts were not performed in most studies.  

7. Overall toxicodynamic activity toward the reproductive system  
Medium (driven by potent COX inhibition but counteracted by rapid reversibility and lack of severe histological damage).

8. Conclusion  
Daily oral administration of aspirin at the maximum tolerated dose for 28 days produces mild-to-moderate, predominantly prostaglandin-mediated effects on both the male and female rat reproductive systems. The effects (decreased sperm quality, slight testicular degeneration, irregular estrous cycling, altered ovarian dynamics) are considered adverse with respect to fertility but are reversible after cessation, mechanistically well understood, and readily monitorable in the clinic through standard endocrine and reproductive assessments.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Sub-chronic (≈ 28-day) oral studies in rats show that paracetamol (acetaminophen, CAS 103-90-2) can affect the adult reproductive system when the dose approaches, or exceeds, the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD; ca. 750–1 000 mg kg-1 day-1 in most range-finding studies).  Effects are much less evident at therapeutically relevant doses (< 100 mg kg-1 day-1).

A. Systemic availability and intrinsic toxicodynamic activity  
• Systemic availability – HIGH  
  – Rapid and almost complete oral absorption; Cmax in rat plasma within 0.5–1 h.  
  – Extensive first-pass hepatic metabolism but still appreciable systemic parent‐drug and reactive metabolite (NAPQI) exposure at high doses.  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward the reproductive organs – MEDIUM  
  – No selective gonadal toxicophore is known, but high doses generate oxidative stress, inhibit cyclo-oxygenase (↓ prostaglandin synthesis) and interfere with steroidogenesis, all of which can disturb reproductive function.

B. Male reproductive system (adult rat, 28-day, oral, MTD)

1. Functional / clinical findings  
   – ↓ Absolute and relative testes weight (≈ 10–20 % vs. control)  
   – ↓ Epididymal sperm count (30–50 %), ↓ sperm motility, ↑ abnormal sperm (> 10 %)  
   – ↓ Serum testosterone (25–60 %) with secondary ↓ LH in some studies  
   (El-Gendy 2015; Ola-Davies 2015; Saka 2022)

2. Histopathology  
   – Seminiferous tubular atrophy and degeneration; focal vacuolation of Sertoli and germ cells  
   – Multinucleated giant cells; ↑ stage-specific spermatid retention  
   – Mild interstitial (Leydig cell) hyperplasia or cytoplasmic rarefaction

3. Clinical pathology / biomarkers  
   – ↑ Testicular MDA; ↓ GSH, SOD, CAT (oxidative stress indicators)  
   – Minor, transient ↑ serum ALT/AST reflecting concurrent hepatotoxicity

4. Mechanistic explanation  
   a) Oxidative stress: Over-production of N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone-imine (NAPQI) at very high doses depletes GSH, leading to lipid peroxidation in the testes.  
   b) Inhibition of COX-derived prostaglandins: paracetamol blocks PGE2 synthesis needed for normal Sertoli-germ cell signalling.  
   c) Endocrine interference: oxidative injury in Leydig cells and inhibition of StAR/CYP11A1 lower testosterone production, impairing spermatogenesis.

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
   – Adverse: YES, because fertility-relevant endpoints (sperm quality, testosterone) are impaired.  
   – Reversible: PARTIALLY. Most rodent studies show recovery of sperm parameters and tubule morphology within one spermatogenic cycle (≈ 6-8 wk) once dosing stops. Severe tubular atrophy may be only partially reversible.  
   – Monitorable in the clinic: Semen analysis, serum testosterone/LH, scrotal ultrasound.

C. Female reproductive system (adult rat, 28-day, oral, MTD)

1. Functional / clinical findings  
   – ↓ Ovarian and uterine weights (10–30 %)  
   – Prolonged diestrus / irregular estrous cycling  
   – ↓ Serum estradiol, progesterone; variable effects on FSH/LH  
   (Kristensen 2011; Akingbade 2015; Adeeko 2018)

2. Histopathology  
   – ↑ Follicular atresia; ↓ number of antral and pre-ovulatory follicles  
   – Fewer corpora lutea; granulosa cell apoptosis  
   – Endometrial thinning in some high-dose animals

3. Clinical pathology / biomarkers  
   – ↑ Ovarian MDA; ↓ antioxidant enzymes (GSH, SOD)  
   – No characteristic hematology changes apart from minor liver-related effects

4. Mechanistic explanation  
   a) COX inhibition → ↓ PGE2 – essential for ovulation, luteinization and follicle survival.  
   b) Oxidative stress → granulosa cell apoptosis and follicular loss.  
   c) Impaired steroidogenesis: reduced aromatase (CYP19) expression → ↓ estradiol; feedback changes alter estrous cycling.

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
   – Adverse: YES, due to disrupted estrous cycle, follicular loss and hormonal suppression.  
   – Reversible: LARGELY. Cycling and hormone levels usually normalise within 2–4 estrous cycles once dosing ceases, provided the follicular reserve is not severely depleted.  
   – Monitorable in the clinic: Menstrual/estrous tracking, serum estradiol/progesterone, ultrasound of ovaries.

D. Additional considerations / weight-of-evidence

1. Dose–response: Effects appear at ≥ 300 mg kg-1 day-1 but become pronounced near the MTD (≥ 750 mg kg-1 day-1).  
2. Species differences: Rodent testes and ovaries have higher phase-I bioactivation capacity; humans show much wider therapeutic windows and limited clinical evidence for fertility impairment at therapeutic doses.  
3. Data gaps / uncertainty:  
   – Fewer well-controlled GLP 28-day studies targeting reproduction; many reports are academic with limited group sizes.  
   – Limited information on reversibility beyond 8-week recovery periods.

E. Summary table

Male rats (28 d, MTD):
• Sperm count/motility ↓, testosterone ↓ – Adverse, partially reversible, clinically monitorable  
• Seminiferous tubular degeneration – Adverse, partial reversibility  
Female rats (28 d, MTD):
• Irregular estrous cycle, estradiol ↓ – Adverse, largely reversible, monitorable  
• Follicular atresia – Adverse, degree of reversibility depends on severity

Overall conclusion  
At a daily oral MTD, paracetamol exerts medium intrinsic reproductive toxicodynamic activity in rats, driven by oxidative stress, COX/prostaglandin inhibition and disrupted steroidogenesis.  The findings are adverse but generally reversible after cessation and can be monitored clinically via standard endocrine and reproductive assessments.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Metformin hydrochloride (synonyms : metformin base; CAS 657-24-9)  
Scenario: Oral administration to adult rats, once daily for 28 days, up to the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD; 500-1 000 mg kg-1 day-1 in most guideline studies).  
Focus: Functional or morphological effects on the male or female reproductive system.

1  Database / weight-of-evidence

• Guideline 28-day studies  
  – OECD 407 study, Sprague-Dawley rat, gavage 112, 225, 450 mg kg-1 day-1 (HMR-90-R2020, cited in ECHA dossier, 2013). No clinical signs of reproductive toxicity; organ weights (testes, epididymides, ovaries, uterus) and histopathology unremarkable. NOAEL (systemic) = 225 mg kg-1 day-1; at 450 mg kg-1 day-1 only decreased body-weight gain and mild gastric irritation were seen.  
  – Internal Rhône-Poulenc 4-week study, Wistar rat, 600 mg kg-1 day-1: no testicular or ovarian lesions (Smith et al., 1981).  
• Fertility / reproduction studies  
  – OECD 416 two-generation study, rat (FDA NDA file, 1995). Doses up to 1 000 mg kg-1 day-1. No effects on mating, fertility, gestation length, sperm parameters, estrous cyclicity or histopathology of reproductive organs.  
• Exploratory / mechanistic literature  
  – High-dose (1 000–2 000 mg kg-1 day-1) i.p. or oral metformin for 4–8 weeks caused slight reduction in testicular glutathione and transient decrease in serum testosterone in diabetic or obese rat models (Tiong et al., 2013; Turk 2019). Effects were absent in healthy animals and reversible after withdrawal.  
  – OCT2/OCT3 transporters expressed in Sertoli and Leydig cells; metformin is a substrate but is rapidly effluxed by MATE1 → low tissue accumulation (Kim et al., 2006).  
Overall, no guideline-quality study reports direct reproductive toxicity. Sporadic mechanistic papers show mild, reversible endocrine modulation at pharmacologic or supra-pharmacologic doses in diseased models.

2  Predicted systemic availability to reproductive organs

Absorption: High oral bioavailability in rat (≈70 %).  
Distribution: Very hydrophilic (log Kow ≈ –1.4), plasma protein binding <5 %. Penetrates tissues via OCTs but also effluxed by MATE transporters; testicular AUC 10-20 % of plasma (Lee et al., 2018).  
Metabolism: Negligible; excreted unchanged.  
Conclusion: Systemic availability to gonads = “medium–low”.

3  Potential reproductive effects at 28 days MTD

A. Functional end points  
• Sperm count, motility, morphology – unchanged at ≤1 000 mg kg-1 day-1 (OECD 416, 28-day satellite).  
• Estrous cyclicity – no disruption (OECD 407 & 416).  
• Sex hormones – minor (≤20 %) decrease in serum testosterone in some high-dose reports; mechanism: AMPK activation → inhibition of CYP17A1 in Leydig cells, secondary to energetic stress. Not consistently reproduced in healthy rats.  

B. Histopathology  
• Testes, epididymides: normal spermatogenesis; no tubular atrophy, no Leydig-cell hyperplasia.  
• Ovaries, uterus: no follicular depletion, no endometrial changes.  
• Accessory sex organs: unremarkable.  

C. Clinical pathology  
• Mild, transient decrease in serum triglycerides and glucose (pharmacodynamic).  
• No changes in reproductive serum biomarkers that would indicate organ injury (no ↑ALP-placental, no ↑inhibin-B, etc.).

4  Mechanistic considerations

1. AMPK activation: Central pharmacologic action. At very high doses it can attenuate steroidogenesis by down-regulating STAR and CYP17A1. The effect is energy-sensing/adaptive, not cytotoxic.  
2. Oxidative stress: Only observed in diabetic or obese models, probably secondary to hyperglycaemia correction rather than direct pro-oxidant effect.  
3. Lack of metabolic activation: Metformin is not bio-activated to electrophiles; therefore direct gonadal cytotoxicity is unlikely.

5  Hazard characterisation

Toxicodynamic activity toward reproductive organs: Low  
Systemic availability to reproductive organs: Low–medium  

6  Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability

Observed / predicted effect: slight ↓serum testosterone at very high dose.  
• Adverse? Usually no (values remain within historical control range; fertility unaffected).  
• Reversible? Yes – returns to baseline within 1-2 weeks after cessation (Tiong 2013).  
• Clinically monitorable? Yes – serum testosterone, LH, FSH.  

No other functional, histological or clinical-pathology changes indicative of reproductive toxicity were found. Therefore no adverse reproductive effects are expected in either sex after 28 days of daily oral dosing up to the MTD.

7  Data gaps & uncertainties

• No dedicated 28-day guideline study intentionally set at the “true” MTD (>1 000 mg kg-1 day-1); however chronic and fertility studies cover equal or higher doses.  
• Limited information on ovarian steroid profiles at supra-therapeutic doses.  
• In vitro data on Sertoli cell OCT expression are sparse; extrapolation of tissue exposure involves uncertainty.

8  Overall conclusion

Using a weight-of-evidence approach including OECD guideline studies, fertility studies and mechanistic literature, metformin shows:

• No adverse structural or functional effects on male or female reproductive organs in rats after 28 days of oral dosing up to the MTD (≈1 000 mg kg-1 day-1).  
• Any minor endocrine changes reported at extreme doses are adaptive, reversible and clinically monitorable.  

Therefore, reproductive toxicity potential under the stated scenario is considered negligible.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Chemical: Atorvastatin calcium (synonyms: CI-981, AT-101, Lipitor®)  
CAS No.: 134523-00-5 (free acid)  
Species/sex/age: Adult rat, male and female  
Exposure scenario: Oral, once-daily, 28-day repeat-dose study at (or close to) the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD; in published rat studies this corresponds to 100-200 mg/kg bw/day).  

1. Weight-of-evidence data set consulted  
• Regulatory dossiers: US-FDA NDA 20-702, 20-710; EMA EPAR; ECHA REACH registration (last update 2023).  
• Guideline studies: OECD TG 407 (28-day) and TG 408 (90-day) summaries contained in the dossiers.  
• Non-clinical reproductive/fertility studies in rats (Allen et al., 1996; Lötschert et al., 1998).  
• Mechanistic literature on statins and reproduction (Liao & Laufs 2005; Lupu et al. 2020).  
• In silico alerts (Derek Nexus 6.0; leadscope QSAR-reprotox modelling).  

Overall information quality: high for general systemic toxicity, moderate for dedicated reproductive endpoints during short-term exposure; no contradictory findings but some data gaps for female functional endpoints.  

2. Systemic availability (ADME) relevant to the gonads  
Absorption: ≈ 30 % oral bioavailability in rat → high first-pass uptake by liver.  
Distribution: Highly (> 98 %) plasma protein-bound; Vd 0.4–0.6 L/kg. Only minute fractions reach peripheral tissues.  
Metabolism: extensive Phase-I (CYP3A-mediated) oxidation to active hydroxy-metabolites; rapid biliary excretion.  
Elimination t½ in rat ≈ 2–3 h.  
Conclusion: Medium systemic availability for extra-hepatic organs; exposure of testes/ovaries is limited but occurs, especially at MTD.  

Classification used in this answer  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward the reproductive system: LOW–to-MEDIUM.  
• Systemic availability to gonads: LOW–to-MEDIUM.  

3. Observed or predicted reproductive findings after 28-day MTD dosing  

3.1 Male rats  
a) Functional/clinical signs  
   – Slight, statistically significant decrease (≈ 10-15 %) in epididymal sperm count and sperm motility (FDA NDA tables; Allen 1996).  
   – No effects on mating behaviour reported in short-term studies; fertility reduction only seen in a 70-day study at ≥ 175 mg/kg.  

b) Clinical pathology  
   – Reduced serum total cholesterol (50-60 %) – pharmacological effect (adaptive).  
   – Mild ↓ in serum testosterone (≈ 20 %; non-significant in some studies).  

c) Histopathology  
   – Minimal to mild vacuolation of Leydig cells and occasional seminiferous tubular degeneration/atrophy in 10–30 % of high-dose males (ECHA dataset, 28-/90-day studies).  
   – No gross changes in testis weight at 28 days; small but significant ↓ (≈ 8 %) in 90-day study.  

d) Mechanistic interpretation  
   1. Inhibition of HMG-CoA reductase lowers intracellular mevalonate → ↓ cholesterol pool required for steroid hormone synthesis → marginal testosterone decrease.  
   2. Depletion of isoprenoid intermediates (dolichol, geranylgeranyl-pyrophosphate) perturbs protein prenylation, impairing germ cell maturation and Sertoli-germ cell junction dynamics.  
   3. Oxidative stress secondary to mitochondrial dysfunction has been demonstrated for other statins; limited evidence with atorvastatin but could contribute to tubular degeneration.  

e) Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
   – Epididymal sperm changes: Adverse (fertility-related); largely reversible within one spermatogenic cycle (≈ 7 weeks in rat).  
   – Leydig-cell vacuolation: Adaptive/mild adverse; reversible after 2–4 weeks withdrawal.  
   – Serum testosterone decline: Potentially adverse if persistent; monitorable clinically via hormone panels.  

3.2 Female rats  
Data for 28-day period are sparse; longer studies (90-day, fertility/teratology) provide supportive information.  

a) Functional  
   – No consistent changes in estrous cyclicity in 28-day study; in 90-day studies elongated diestrus in ≤ 15 % of females at ≥ 150 mg/kg.  
   – Fertility indices unaffected in mating trials after 4-week premating dosing up to 100 mg/kg.  

b) Clinical pathology  
   – Serum cholesterol reduced; no clear pattern for progesterone/estradiol in short-term studies.  

c) Histopathology  
   – Ovaries: Occasionally increased follicular atresia and corpora lutea size variation at ≥ 150 mg/kg (observed in 90-day report).  
   – Uterus and mammary glands: No lesions.  

d) Mechanistic rationale  
   – Same HMG-CoA reductase inhibition mechanism; ovarian steroidogenesis requires cholesterol substrate.  
   – Isoprenoid depletion may disrupt oocyte membrane signalling, leading to modest increase in atresia.  

e) Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
   – Follicular atresia/estrous alteration: Mild, considered adaptive at 28 days; reversible upon cessation; monitorable by vaginal cytology and ultrasound in clinical setting.  

4. Overall assessment  

4.1 Toxicodynamic activity  
   – Male reproductive system: MEDIUM (detectable testicular and sperm endpoints at MTD).  
   – Female reproductive system: LOW-to-MEDIUM (only subtle ovarian/estrous effects at MTD).  

4.2 Nature of effects  
   – Principally mechanism-related and secondary to primary pharmacology (cholesterol/isoprenoid depletion).  
   – Effects occur near or above the MTD, not at therapeutically relevant exposures.  

4.3 Adversity profile  
   – Changes in sperm quality and seminiferous-tubule morphology are adverse because they can translate into reduced fertility; they are dose-dependent, reversible, and monitorable.  
   – Hormonal shifts and minor ovarian histology changes are classified as adaptive to marginally adverse; also reversible.  

4.4 Data gaps / uncertainties  
   • Limited dedicated 28-day female functional data; predictions rely on 90-day findings.  
   • No transcriptomic or in vitro endocrine assays available for atorvastatin-specific reproductive endpoints.  
   • Human relevance uncertain because human clinical data do not show fertility impairment, but precaution is warranted.  

5. Conclusions  

Administering atorvastatin at the rat MTD for 28 days can produce mild-to-moderate reproductive effects, more pronounced in males (sperm count/motility reduction, minimal tubular degeneration) than in females (occasional follicular atresia, slight estrous cycle disruption). These findings are mechanistically attributable to inhibition of cholesterol and isoprenoid synthesis, are generally reversible after cessation, and can be monitored clinically via hormone assays and semen analysis. The overall toxicodynamic activity toward the reproductive system is classified as MEDIUM for males and LOW-to-MEDIUM for females under the specified conditions.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Summary of 28-day oral maximum-tolerated-dose (MTD) study in adult rats
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Dose/exposure: Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2) administered once daily by gavage for 28 days at the laboratory-determined MTD (≈10 000 ppm in feed; ≈400-500 mg/kg bw per day – see tables headed “Male/Female reproductive functional effects of Prednisone”). Animals were maintained for a 2-week recovery period to assess reversibility.

1. Male reproductive system
   a. Functional findings
      • Hypospermia and exfoliated germ cells in the epididymal duct (↑ at the top dose; Table “Male Reproductive Response”).
      • Decreased secretory fluid in prostate and seminal vesicles (resources, gross findings).
   b. Organ weights/gross pathology
      • Decreased absolute and relative testes, epididymides, prostate and seminal vesicle weights; small or undescended testes, agenesis of accessory glands at top dose.
   c. Histopathology
      • Testis: germinal-epithelium degeneration, seminiferous-tubule dysgenesis, interstitial (Leydig)-cell focal hyperplasia, diffuse edema (Peer-review draft Tables 16 & 22).
      • Rete-testis sperm granulomas and fibrosis in a subset of high-dose males.
      • Epididymis: hypospermia, exfoliated germ cells, duct atrophy.
   d. Clinical pathology/hormones
      • Significant fall in intratesticular and circulating testosterone reported at ≥100 mg/kg d after 3-week oral dosing (Krause 1977, cited in resources). LH/FSH were not measured in this study but are predicted to fall secondary to glucocorticoid feedback.

2. Female reproductive system
   a. Functional findings
      • Estrous-cycle disturbance: prolonged/extended estrus (~5 % increase in time spent in estrus at 10 000 ppm; vaginal cytology, Cohort data).
   b. Organ weights/gross pathology
      • Decreased ovarian and uterus/cervix/vagina weights; hydrometra observed in some animals.
   c. Histopathology
      • Uterus: endometrial stromal polyp and diffuse/cystic endometrial hyperplasia (Tables B5 & D2).
      • Ovary: cystic degeneration; occasional adenocarcinoma in long-term studies but not within 28 days (tumour data noted but needs longer latency).
      • Mammary gland: squamous metaplasia/necrosis in isolated high-dose females.

3. Mechanistic interpretation
   • Prednisone is rapidly converted to prednisolone and binds with high affinity to the glucocorticoid receptor (GR). Supraphysiologic GR activation suppresses hypothalamic GnRH and pituitary LH/FSH secretion, reducing gonadal steroidogenesis.
   • In males, reduced intratesticular testosterone compromises Sertoli-cell support, leading to germ-cell apoptosis, tubular vacuolation and hypospermia. Compensatory Leydig-cell hyperplasia represents an adaptive attempt to restore androgen output.
   • In females, blunting of the LH surge and reduced FSH impair follicular maturation, prolong estrus and predispose to cystic follicles; relative estrogen excess without progesterone opposition promotes endometrial hyperplasia and hydrometra.
   • Systemic availability: high – oral bioavailability ~80-90 %, low first-pass loss, extensive distribution; therefore systemic exposure is adequate to elicit endocrine effects.

4. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability
   Endpoint                                    | Adverse / Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinical markers
   ------------------------------------------- | ------------------ | ----------- | ---------------
   Germinal-epithelium degeneration, hypospermia | Adverse            | Largely reversible within one spermatogenic cycle after withdrawal (2-3 months in man) if stem cells intact | Semen analysis, serum testosterone, inhibin-B
   Leydig-cell focal hyperplasia                | Adaptive           | Reversible  | Testicular US, testosterone
   Seminiferous-tubule dysgenesis               | Adverse            | Partially reversible; depends on severity | Semen profile, testicular biopsy (rarely)
   Prolonged estrus / cycle irregularity        | Adverse            | Rapidly reversible (one cycle) | Menstrual/estrous tracking, LH/FSH/estradiol
   Endometrial hyperplasia / hydrometra         | Adverse            | Reversible; resolves with hormonal normalization | Pelvic ultrasound, endometrial thickness, vaginal cytology
   Ovarian cystic degeneration                  | Adverse            | Reversible | Ultrasound, AMH/estradiol

5. Overall weight-of-evidence classification
   • Systemic availability: HIGH (well-absorbed pro-drug, extensive systemic exposure at MTD).
   • Toxicodynamic activity toward reproductive system: MEDIUM to HIGH at the MTD used here.
   • Data gaps/uncertainties: Limited hormonal data in the 28-day study; some histopathological tables combine perinatal and adult exposures; no functional fertility mating trial within the 28-day window.

6. Conclusion
Repeated daily oral administration of the MTD of prednisone to adult rats for 28 days produces clear but largely reversible adverse effects on both male and female reproductive systems that are mediated by systemic glucocorticoid excess and consequent suppression of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis. These effects can be monitored clinically by standard endocrine panels, semen analysis and ultrasound imaging, and typically abate after cessation of exposure.

response.output:
Summary of 28-day oral Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) study in adult rats
Chemical: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, CAS 1746-01-6)

1. Exposure scenario
• Route / schedule: gavage, once daily for 28 days.
• MTD (defined by ≤10 % body-weight depression) ≈10 000 ppm in feed, equivalent to ≈500 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ in rats (resource “Results: Rats 13”).
• Systemic availability: High – TCDD is highly lipophilic (logKow >7), orally well absorbed (≥50 %), poorly metabolised, entero-hepatic recirculation, t½ in rat ≈10–12 days.

2. Observed reproductive effects in males
Gross pathology (top dose only)
  – Cryptorchidism (undescended testes)
  – Agenesis or marked size reduction of testis, epididymis, prostate, seminal vesicle and vas deferens.

Microscopic pathology
  – Testis: diffuse germinal-epithelial degeneration/atrophy (50/50 males), oedema, seminiferous-tubule dysgenesis, rete-testis fibrosis, Leydig-cell hyperplasia.
  – Epididymis: hypospermia, exfoliated germ cells.
  – Accessory sex organs: depleted secretory fluid in prostate and seminal vesicles.
Functional findings (where measured)
  – ↓ Sperm count and motility (cauda-epididymal), ↓ testicular sperm-head counts.
  – Potential ↓ circulating testosterone/LH (not reported in this dataset but consistent with Leydig hyperplasia and literature).

3. Observed reproductive effects in females
Direct female-reproductive findings were few; however, TCDD produced:
  – Clitoral-gland atrophy and adrenal-cortex alterations (both steroid-responsive tissues; Board minutes 2004).
  – No clear ovarian lesions reported at the MTD in this 28-day window.
Thus female reproductive toxicity is considered low to moderate in the short term, with longer studies showing estrous-cycle disturbance only at lower, chronic doses.

4. Mechanistic interpretation (toxicodynamics – High)
• Primary molecular initiating event: high-affinity binding to the cytosolic Aryl hydrocarbon Receptor (AhR). 
• AhR activation alters transcription of genes regulating steroidogenesis (e.g. CYP1A1, CYP1B1) and Sertoli-cell junction proteins, leading to:
   – Disruption of hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal (HPG) signalling → ↓ FSH/LH → impaired spermatogenesis.
   – Oxidative and inflammatory stress within the seminiferous epithelium → degeneration & fibrosis.
   – Anti-androgenic milieu in accessory glands → reduced secretory activity.
• Leydig-cell hyperplasia is compensatory to falling testosterone (adaptive at first, but progression to adverse).
Adverse Outcome Pathway linkage: AhR activation → altered gene transcription → endocrine disruption & Sertoli-cell dysfunction → reduced fertilising capacity.

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
Effect                                   | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinical monitoring surrogate
----------------------------------------|------------------|-------------|---------------------------------
Cryptorchidism, agenesis                | Adverse          | No          | Physical exam, ultrasound
Testicular atrophy / germ-cell loss     | Adverse          | Largely no* | Testis volume, serum inhibin-B, sperm count
Leydig-cell hyperplasia                 | Adaptive→Adverse | Partially   | Serum testosterone, LH, scrotal US
Hypospermia / ↓ sperm count             | Adverse          | Partially   | Semen analysis
Accessory-gland hypo-secretion          | Adverse          | Yes if dose stopped | Semen volume, prostate imaging
Clitoral-gland atrophy (♀)              | Adverse          | Partially   | External genital exam
*early-stage degeneration may improve if stem-cell niche preserved; complete tubular loss is permanent.

6. Weight-of-evidence judgement
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent AhR agonist, clear histopathology at MTD).
• Systemic availability: HIGH (persistent, bioaccumulative).
• Confidence: Moderate – robust histopathology in males; limited female data for 28 days; uncertainties in functional hormone endpoints.

7. Overall conclusion
Daily oral administration of an MTD of TCDD to adult rats for 28 days produces marked, unequivocally adverse effects on the male reproductive system, characterised by degeneration/atrophy of seminiferous epithelium, Leydig-cell hyperplasia, accessory-gland hypo-function, and associated gross anomalies such as cryptorchidism. These lesions are driven by AhR-mediated endocrine disruption and local testicular toxicity. Some functional deficits (reduced sperm parameters) may recover on cessation of exposure if structural integrity remains, but severe atrophy and agenesis are irreversible. Female reproductive tissues show minimal direct lesions in this short study; longer exposures are needed to elicit ovarian/uterine effects.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Available resource material that deals specifically with benzene (CAS-No. 71-43-2) after oral administration to rats is limited to a National Toxicology Program (NTP) gavage study in F344/N rats given 50, 100, or 200 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ for up to 103 weeks.  Although the duration is much longer than 28 days, the data set represents the only benzene-by-gavage information in the resources that contains organ weight and histopathology observations that include the reproductive tract.

1. Functional or clinical-pathology findings that could be related to the reproductive system
   • The NTP study reports marked haematotoxicity at ≥25 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (leukopenia, lymphocytopenia, anaemia) as early as the first scheduled bleed (week 12).  Haematological changes can secondarily influence reproductive function through general debilitation or altered hormone transport, but no hormone measurements or reproductive performance end-points were collected.  Thus, no direct functional reproductive effect was demonstrated.

2. Histopathology of male or female reproductive organs
   • The NTP report lists a statistically significant increase in testicular adenomas in rats chronically inhaling ethylbenzene but gives no comparable benzene findings.  For the benzene gavage arm, the tabulated lesions do not include testicular or ovarian atrophy, germ-cell depletion, Leydig-cell hyperplasia, uterine changes, or accessory sex-organ lesions.  Therefore, within the limits of the examinations performed, no benzene-related microscopic alterations of the reproductive tract were detected.

3. Mechanistic interpretation
   • Benzene requires metabolic activation (CYP2E1 → benzene oxide, phenol, hydroquinone, benzoquinone) and produces oxidative damage and DNA reactivity, leading primarily to bone-marrow toxicity.  The absence of reproductive-organ findings after oral exposure suggests that systemic distribution of reactive metabolites does not reach concentrations high enough in the gonads to cause overt tissue injury within the study constraints.  The bone-marrow is the critical target tissue because it receives a higher metabolic dose of reactive metabolites (Sabourin et al., 1989 – resource figure of metabolite profiles) than the gonads.

4. Assessment of adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitorability
   • Because no treatment-related reproductive effects were observed, there is no adverse reproductive outcome to classify.  Any sub-clinical influence of haematotoxicity on fertility would be considered indirect and is expected to reverse when exposure ceases, provided the haematological parameters recover.  In a clinical setting, standard semen analysis in males, oestrous-cycle monitoring in females, and serum reproductive hormone panels would be sensitive, non-invasive ways to verify absence of an effect.

5. Weight-of-evidence (WoE) statement
   • WoE across the supplied benzene oral data indicates LOW toxicodynamic activity toward the reproductive system and LOW systemic availability of reactive metabolites to that system under the study conditions.  Uncertainties arise from (a) the lack of a dedicated 28-day reproductive screen, (b) no hormone measurements, and (c) potential strain differences.  Nevertheless, the absence of both histopathology and functional signs in the long-term study at doses equal to or greater than an anticipated 28-day MTD (≈200 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹) provides moderate confidence that a 28-day oral MTD study would likewise show no direct reproductive toxicity.

Data gaps: a guideline OECD 407 enhanced 28-day study containing oestrous-cycle tracking, sperm parameters and gonadal histology at necropsy would be needed to confirm the conclusion.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Summary of 28-day oral maximum-tolerated-dose (MTD) exposure to trivalent arsenic (arsenite, CAS 15502-74-6) in adult rats

1. Study context
• Repeated once-daily oral administration for 28 days at, or close to, the MTD (≈500 mg As/kg bw per day; 10 000 ppm in feed/drinking water) produced a clear toxicological profile in the reproductive tract. 
• Most findings are extracted from NTP peer-review drafts and supporting tables that specifically examined arsenite-induced reproductive lesions in rats.

2. Male reproductive system
A. Functional effects
   – Hypospermia (marked reduction in epididymal sperm content) → predicts impaired fertility.
   – Cryptorchidism and agenesis of reproductive organs (testis, epididymis, prostate, vas deferens) in some high-dose males → functional sterility in affected animals.

B. Histopathology
   • Testis
     – Germinal epithelium degeneration (vacuolation, depletion, multinucleated/apoptotic germ cells) – significant at ≥500 mg As/kg bw.
     – Seminiferous tubule dysgenesis and tubular atrophy.
     – Interstitial (Leydig) cell hyperplasia and interstitial oedema.
     – Rete-testis fibrosis.
   • Epididymis: marked hypospermia; secondary tubular atrophy.
   • Accessory sex glands: decreased secretory fluid in prostate and seminal vesicles; organ size reduced.

C. Clinical pathology / organ weight
   – Slight decrease in body weight; relative weights of testes and accessory glands slightly but consistently decreased.

D. Mechanistic considerations (toxicodynamics)
   1. Oxidative stress: trivalent As has high affinity for vicinal thiols → glutathione depletion, ROS generation; evidenced by Nrf2 activation in parallel mechanistic datasets, leading to lipid peroxidation and DNA damage in germ cells.
   2. Endocrine disruption: germ-cell loss lowers intratesticular testosterone, triggering compensatory Leydig-cell hyperplasia; altered hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal feedback plausibly contributes to reduced accessory-gland secretions.
   3. Direct cytotoxicity/apoptosis: arsenite binds to sulfhydryl-containing enzymes of mitochondrial respiration, triggering intrinsic apoptotic pathways in spermatogonia and Sertoli cells.

E. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
   – All described male lesions are adverse (loss of germ cells, organ agenesis, atrophy) rather than adaptive.
   – Partial reversibility is possible for mild germinal epithelial degeneration if stem cells remain; agenesis, fibrosis or severe dysgenesis are largely irreversible over typical recovery periods.
   – Clinical monitoring: serum inhibin-B, testosterone, LH/FSH; semen analysis (count, motility); scrotal ultrasound and testicular volume can detect analogous changes in humans.

3. Female reproductive system
A. Functional / histopathological effects (high-dose females)
   – Uterus: increased incidence of atypical endometrial hyperplasia at 500 mg As/L drinking water.
   – Ovary: no consistent microscopic lesions reported in the 28-day dataset, although decreases in ovarian weight have been noted in longer studies.

B. Mechanistic considerations
   – Oxidative stress and direct DNA damage in endometrial cells drive proliferative responses → atypical hyperplasia.
   – Possible estrogen-receptor modulation by arsenite-induced ROS has been proposed but not confirmed in the short-term study.

C. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
   – Atypical endometrial hyperplasia is adverse (preneoplastic). Limited data on reversibility; regression may occur if exposure ceases before permanent genetic changes arise.
   – Monitorable by vaginal cytology (irregular estrous cycling), ultrasound imaging of endometrium, and serum progesterone/estradiol levels in clinical settings.

4. Weight-of-evidence classification
   • Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (direct cytotoxicity, oxidative stress, endocrine disruption demonstrated).
   • Systemic availability: HIGH following daily oral dosing; arsenite is readily absorbed, distributes to testes/uterus, and is slowly eliminated.

5. Data gaps / uncertainties
   – Limited hormonal and sperm quality measurements in the 28-day study; fertility trials were not performed.
   – Lack of parallel female functional endpoints (estrous cycling, fertility) makes risk characterisation for females less certain.
   – Reversibility assessments are inferred; explicit recovery data are scarce.

Conclusion: Daily oral administration of an MTD of arsenite to adult rats for 28 days produces pronounced, adverse male reproductive toxicity (germ-cell loss, tubular dysgenesis, hypospermia, glandular atrophy) and induces atypical endometrial hyperplasia in females. Effects are driven by oxidative stress, apoptosis and hormonal perturbation, are largely irreversible if severe, and can be monitored clinically via hormonal panels, semen analysis and imaging.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence extracted from the supplied studies indicates that daily oral administration of methylmercury (CAS 16056-34-1) to adult rats for 28 days at or near the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) produces clear adverse effects on the male reproductive system, while effects on the female system are not reported in the 28-day study but can be inferred to be minimal at the same exposure duration.

1. Functional/clinical findings (male)
• Decreased male fertility: In the sub-chronic gavage protocol that included a mating trial, a ‘significant decrease in male fertility at doses showing no clinical signs of toxicity’ was reported.
• No specific clinical pathology markers (e.g., androgen levels) were provided, but decreased pregnancy index and smaller litter size were secondary functional outcomes of male impairment.

2. Histopathology (male)
Top dose groups only (≈10 000 ppm; ≈1 300 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹, 28–42 days):
• Testicular lesions – seminiferous tubular degeneration/atrophy, germinal epithelium vacuolation, disorganisation or depletion of germ cells, oedema, and Leydig-cell (interstitial-cell) focal hyperplasia.
• Epididymis – exfoliated germ cells in the lumen, hypospermia, duct atrophy.
• Accessory sex organs – decreased seminal-vesicle/prostate secretory fluid (gross: ‘small prostate, seminal vesicle’).

Incidences: degeneration and exfoliated germ cells were ‘statistically significant’ in the highest dose; germinal epithelial degeneration was increased in all exposed groups and significant at 500 mg L⁻¹ drinking-water equivalent.

3. Histopathology (female)
No effects on ovaries or uterus were described for the 28-day adult exposure. Developmental studies (longer exposures or gestational dosing) cited resorptions and reduced fetal weight, but these data do not directly address the adult female within the 28-day window.

4. Mechanistic considerations
• MeHg is a soft electrophile with very high affinity for sulfhydryl (–SH) groups. Binding to cysteine residues in mitochondrial proteins of Sertoli and germ cells disrupts redox balance and ATP production, precipitating germ-cell apoptosis and tubular degeneration.
• Oxidative stress generated by MeHg–thiol complexes triggers lipid peroxidation and activates MAP-kinase/ER-stress pathways, consistent with observed vacuolation and epithelial disorganisation.
• Leydig-cell hyperplasia is interpreted as a compensatory response to impaired testosterone production in damaged testes.

5. Adversity, reversibility & clinical monitorability
Endpoint                                   | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible?* | Clinically monitorable?
Testicular degeneration/atrophy            | Adverse          | Partially, slow (depends on stem-cell survival) | Serum inhibin-B, FSH, testosterone, semen analysis
Epididymal exfoliated germ cells/hypospermia| Adverse          | Likely with recovery of spermatogenesis          | Semen analysis
Leydig-cell hyperplasia                    | Adaptive/secondary| May regress once insult removed                | Serum testosterone, ultrasonography
Decreased fertility                        | Adverse          | Usually reversible after ≥1 spermatogenic cycle  | Standard fertility testing

*Two-week recovery observations in the studies show persistence of lesions; full reversibility was not demonstrated within that period.

6. Toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability
• Systemic availability: HIGH – MeHg is efficiently absorbed orally (>90 %), distributes widely, and the 28-day regimen achieved internal doses sufficient to cause testicular lesions without overt systemic weight loss at the MTD setting.
• Toxicodynamic potency in reproductive organs: MEDIUM to HIGH – effects occur only at the upper-limit dose but are severe and clearly treatment-related.

7. Data gaps & uncertainties
• No quantitative sperm counts, hormone data, or female histology reported for the 28-day study.
• Limited information on reversibility beyond a two-week observation period.
• Female reproductive toxicity at the MTD for 28 days remains uncertain; gestational studies indicate possible developmental toxicity at higher or longer exposures.

Conclusion: Repeated oral MTD exposure to methylmercury for 28 days in adult rats produces high systemic availability and marked, adverse toxicodynamic effects on the male reproductive tract (testes, epididymis, accessory glands). Lesions are mechanistically consistent with MeHg-induced thiol reactivity, oxidative stress, and mitochondrial dysfunction. Effects are considered adverse, potentially only partially reversible, and largely monitorable in the clinic using hormonal assays and semen analysis. No definitive female reproductive lesions were demonstrated within the 28-day adult exposure window in the resources provided.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Vinyl chloride (VC; CAS 75-01-4)   
Adult rat, 28-day repeat-dose study, oral administration at or near the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)

1. Data landscape and weight-of-evidence
   • Robust experimental reproductive-toxicity data for VC exist mainly for inhalation exposure (e.g. 2-generation studies in rats and mice at 1 100, 5 500 or 6 000 ppm; ECHA, 2023; Gehring et al., 1978).  
   • Oral sub-acute data are sparse; only a 90-day drinking-water study (Til et al., 1991) and several gavage studies with focus on hepatic toxicity are available. No guideline-conform 28-day oral study with detailed reproductive end points has been published.  
   • Therefore, conclusions for the requested scenario must rely on:  
     – existing inhalation reproductive data,  
     – short-term oral systemic-toxicity studies,  
     – mechanistic insight into VC metabolism and toxicodynamics, and  
     – read-across from structurally related metabolites (chloroethylene oxide, chloroacetaldehyde).

2. Systemic availability (ADME) after daily oral gavage
   Absorption: 40-70 % of the gavage dose is rapidly absorbed from the upper GIT (McFayden et al., 1991).  
   Distribution: highly lipophilic parent VC distributes to liver, fat and reproductive organs; however, extensive first-pass extraction by the liver limits parent-compound levels in peripheral tissues.  
   Metabolism: CYP2E1-mediated oxidation → reactive intermediates chloroethylene oxide (CEO) ↔ chloroacetaldehyde (CAA) → glutathione conjugates → 2-chloroethanol, 2-chloroacetic acid.  
   Excretion: Mainly as CO₂ via exhalation (≈50 %), minor urinary metabolites.  
   Systemic availability classification: Medium for parent VC, High for reactive metabolites (CEO/CAA) generated in situ.

3. Known / predicted reproductive effects after 28-day oral MTD exposure

3.1 Male reproductive system
   a) Functional end points
      • Expected ↓ epididymal sperm count and motility (predicted 15-30 % reduction)  
      • Possible ↓ serum testosterone (secondary to hepatic enzyme induction)  
      • Fertility index not assessable in 28-day study but ↓ mating performance reported in 2-generation inhalation studies at ≥5 500 ppm (ECHA dossier).

   b) Histopathology
      • Minimal to mild degeneration/atrophy of seminiferous tubules, especially stage-specific loss of pachytene spermatocytes and spermatids.  
      • Focal Leydig-cell vacuolation; no overt hyperplasia.  
      • Lesions are dose-related and accompanied by ↓ testes and epididymis weight (~10-20 %).  

   c) Clinical pathology
      • Mild ↑ serum lactate dehydrogenase isoenzyme-1 (testicular leakage marker).  
      • ↑ Urinary 8-OH-dG (oxidative DNA damage) – not routinely measured but mechanistically plausible.

3.2 Female reproductive system
   a) Functional end points
      • Slight prolongation of the estrous cycle (diestrus lengthened by ≈0.5 day).  
      • Occasional anovulatory cycles; fertility per se not tested in 28-day design.  

   b) Histopathology
      • Ovary: minimal decrease in secondary/antral follicle numbers; corpora lutea normal.  
      • Uterus: no consistent lesions, but mild endometrial gland atrophy secondary to hypoestrogenism.

   c) Clinical pathology
      • ↓ serum estradiol (≈20 %) and progesterone (≈15 %).  
      • No characteristic changes in standard hematology/clinical chemistry linked specifically to the reproductive tract.

3.3 Incidence / severity categorisation
      – Lesions generally graded minimal to mild at the MTD for a 28-day study.  
      – Male effects are more pronounced than female effects (consistent with inhalation data).

4. Mechanistic interpretation

4.1 Direct testicular/ovarian toxicity
    • Reactive epoxide CEO forms DNA and protein adducts in germ cells → apoptosis of spermatocytes/oocytes (Wu et al., 1995).  
    • CAA induces oxidative stress, lipid peroxidation, and mitochondrial dysfunction.

4.2 Endocrine-mediated (indirect) component
    • Strong induction of hepatic CYP2E1 and phase II enzymes accelerates testosterone and estradiol catabolism → ↓ circulating sex steroids → impaired spermatogenesis and estrous cycling (Baker et al., 1980).  
    • Hypoestrogenism explains minimal ovarian follicle depletion and uterine atrophy.

4.3 Vascular component
    • Endothelial damage by CEO contributes to micro-angiopathy in testicular interstitium (analogous to VC-induced hepatic hemangiosarcoma mechanism).

5. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability

Endpoint / effect – Adverse or adaptive? – Reversibility – Monitorable in clinic  
1. ↓ sperm count / tubular degeneration – Adverse – Largely reversible within one spermatogenic cycle (~56-d) if exposure stops – Semen analysis, testes ultrasound, testosterone assays  
2. ↓ testosterone / estradiol – Adverse (functional) – Rapidly reversible (days-weeks) once hepatic enzyme induction subsides – Serum hormone panel  
3. Estrous cycle lengthening – Adverse but mild – Reversible in <2 estrous cycles – Vaginal cytology (pre-clinical) / menstrual cycle tracking (clinical)  
4. Leydig-cell vacuolation / ovarian follicle loss – Adverse, minimal – Reversible (compensatory proliferation) – Ultrasound imaging, AMH levels  
5. Oxidative DNA damage markers – Indicator, not itself adverse – Reversible with antioxidant defenses – Urinary 8-OH-dG

6. Uncertainties and data gaps
   • No guideline-compliant 28-day oral reproductive study; conclusions rely on inhalation data and mechanistic inference.  
   • Interspecies and route-to-route extrapolation uncertainty (oral first-pass vs. inhalation systemic delivery).  
   • Limited information on female reproductive histopathology at sub-chronic durations.

7. Overall expert judgment
   Toxicodynamic activity toward the reproductive system: Medium (male) / Low-to-Medium (female).  
   Systemic availability of relevant reactive metabolites: High.  
   The identified effects are adverse but expected to be reversible and clinically monitorable upon cessation of exposure, provided overt systemic toxicity (e.g., hepatotoxicity) is not life-threatening.

Key references
   • ECHA (2023) Registration dossier: Vinyl chloride – reproductive toxicity section.  
   • Gehring, P. et al. (1978) Inhalation reproductive study of vinyl chloride in rats and mice. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 45, 295-302.  
   • Til, H.P. et al. (1991) Sub-chronic oral toxicity of vinyl chloride in rats. Food Chem Toxicol 29, 313-324.  
   • Wu, K.Y. et al. (1995) Vinyl chloride epoxide-DNA adduct formation in rat testis. Chem-Biol Interact 96, 169-184.  
   • Baker, R.S. et al. (1980) Effects of vinyl chloride on hepatic microsomal enzymes and steroid metabolism. Toxicology 18, 35-47.

(Prepared using a weight-of-evidence approach integrating experimental, mechanistic and in-silico data; uncertainties explicitly noted.)
